The line "he rowed out into the middle of the most enormous tenderness the world had ever held and was utterly, perfectly alone in it" is the chapter's thesis statement, and it's earned. The cruelty and the awe are genuinely simultaneous here, not alternating. **Weaknesses and Concerns** The chapter is not without vulnerabilities. The opening sentence — "it was the most beautiful thing she would ever be near, and she would spend the rest of her life unable to tell anyone why it had felt, at the same time, like watching her own death from a great distance" — is slightly over-announced. It tells us how to feel before the chapter has shown us why, and the "watching her own death from a great distance" construction is a touch familiar. This is a minor complaint against an otherwise disciplined piece. The phrase "two vastnesses, one ancient and slow and only now awake to itself and dying of being awake" is the chapter's most ambitious sentence and mostly succeeds, though "dying of being awake" is the one moment where the metaphor strains slightly — it's evocative but not quite precise. The Marsh boy section is the chapter's most daring structural choice — introducing a new character at the climax purely as a vessel for a theme — and it works, though it risks feeling schematic. The writer gets away with it because the boy is rendered with genuine pathos rather than as a mere symbol. **Dialogue** There is almost no dialogue, which is the right choice. Garf's shouted words ("what do you want," "what did you do," "answer me, you bastard") function as action rather than conversation, and they're exactly right — the words of a man demanding that the universe be accountable to him. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter executes its plan with exceptional fidelity. The plan called for: the speaking resolving into its fullest form, it being unmistakably not for us, Dot praying, Garf shouting, someone taking a boat out, nothing acknowledging any of them, the cruelty and awe of witnessing immense tenderness between two things that have no use for you, and Maren stopping trying to interpret and simply listening. Every single element is present and handled with more grace than the plan's prose description suggested. **Overall Assessment** This is a chapter that achieves what it sets out to do at a high level of craft. The central emotional and thematic payload — the experience of being incidental to something vast and grieving — is delivered without sentimentality, without false comfort, and without the purple excess that would undermine it. The prose earns its lyrical moments. The characters behave like themselves. The plan is followed and exceeded. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight: After The structure was gone by morning. Not departed in any way she saw—no light, no rising, no great event for the dozen of them to witness and tell each other about later. It had thinned through the night to almost nothing, and at some point before the grey came up it had finished thinning, and where it had been over the heads for thirty-one days there was only the horizon, the plain old horizon she'd stopped being able to see and now could not stop seeing, the way she couldn't stop looking at the empty daybed. The Marsh boy came back with the dawn. He rowed in slow and sat a long while at the slipway not getting out of the boat, and when Garf went down to him he wouldn't say where he'd been or what, out there in the dark in the middle of it, he'd found. He never did say. He went up to his mother's house and after a few days he went up the peninsula on foot, alone, and that was the last of him in Cape Joyce, and Maren didn't blame him. She understood the wanting to be somewhere the thing had never been. The sound had stopped. That was the strangest to get used to—stranger than the silence after Eli, because she'd had four months' warning of that one. The water lay quiet. No teeth, no long bones, no pressure in the chest. The fish stopped dying. She went down on the second morning and there were no new ones in the windrow, and on the third no ranks at all, the tide piling the old dead any-which-way now, tangled, ordinary, the way a tide should, and she stood on the slipway and found that she missed the ranks, which frightened her, and she made herself stop standing there. The fish did not come back. That was the thing she'd have told anyone who asked, if anyone had been left to ask, if any of the stories had had room in them for it. Dot's third angel had a trumpet and a vengeance and a sea turned to blood; it did not have *and then the water was simply empty and quiet and that was all*. Garf's Americans had a weapon and could be cursed and might one day be made to pay; they did not have *and no one ever came to explain*. Pradeep's vast slow mind had grieved and been kept company and—what? Healed? Died? Gone back under into the silence it had spent its bodies to climb out of? She had no way to know and there was no one to tell her and no instrument that would ever measure it. The sea was just sea again. Maybe it was getting well. Maybe it had said what it had stayed alive to say and let go. Maybe it had never been a mind at all and they'd all of them, to the last, made it one because the alternative was unbearable. She would not know. That was the shape of it. She would go to her own grave in the yard above the church not knowing, and so would everyone, and the not-knowing did not resolve into anything. It just went on being the not-knowing. She stayed. There was no decision in it, no vigil-keeping, no taking-up of a noble watch by the side of the changed sea. She stayed because she had nowhere else and never had, same as before, except that now there was a fresh grave in the long grass and a folded blanket she couldn't make herself unfold and the whole emptied house that had been, for four bad months, the one place she was needed. A few of the others drifted back through the summer, when it was clear nothing further was coming—a couple from up the shore, the Pikes' son to fetch Dot at last, Dot going this time, smaller, quieter, the certainty gone out of her like air out of a thing. Garf stayed. Garf would always stay, with his dogs and his punt and his fence, his half of a feud now with no one to feud against, and they took to having a drop in the evenings, the two of them, not talking about it, talking about the weather and the dogs and whether the cod might, in some year neither of them would see, come back to a clean cold empty sea. This morning she made toast. There was power again, intermittent—a crew had come through and got the line half-mended, the country reaching out a hand into the bays now that the thing was over, now that it was safe to remember the small dying places existed. So there was a toaster, and bread, and she put a slice in and stood at the window while it went, looking at the water, the plain flat ordinary water with the morning coming up gold on it the way it had every morning of her childhood, indifferent then and indifferent now and somehow she could bear the indifference better than she'd thought she could. She burned the toast. On purpose. She didn't decide to and then she had, the dial turned up past where it wanted, and when it came out dark she took the knife and scraped it, burnt-then-scraped, his mother's way, the way he'd wanted it and complained about, and she stood there with the black crumbs falling into the sink and her eyes stinging at last, four months late, scraping toast for a dead man over a sea that had grieved something it could never tell her and gone quiet again. She caught herself listening. Just for a moment—head tilted, the old lean toward the wall, the body still hoping for the pressure in the teeth, the turn and the pause, to be near the murmur of it one more time. The water gave nothing back. There was no one in the next room and nothing out past the heads and the conversation, whatever it had been, was over, and had never once been hers. She ate the toast standing up. Then she went out to scrape the slipway, out of habit, because the dead still came in on the tide, fewer now, and someone had to.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This final chapter is a remarkable piece of controlled, deliberate writing that largely succeeds at its most difficult ambition: ending without resolution, without comfort, without the cosmic event redeeming the personal grief or vice versa. Let me examine it carefully. **Strengths:** The opening paragraph is excellent. "It had thinned through the night to almost nothing" — the structure's departure is rendered as absence rather than event, which is exactly right. The comparison to "the empty daybed" is earned and precise, not decorative. The Marsh boy episode is a small, perfect detail: a character we haven't met who rows in, won't speak, and leaves — a human response to the inexplicable that is neither heroic nor cowardly, just broken and ordinary. This is the kind of detail that makes a fictional world feel real. The paragraph about what the fish not returning means — working through Dot's framework, Garf's framework, Pradeep's framework, and finding all of them insufficient — is the thematic heart of the story and it's handled with real intelligence. "She would go to her own grave in the yard above the church not knowing, and so would everyone, and the not-knowing did not resolve into anything. It just went on being the not-knowing." This is the story's thesis stated plainly, and it works because it's been earned across eight chapters of refusing consolation. The toast scene is the chapter's emotional climax and it's beautifully executed. "Burnt-then-scraped, his mother's way" — this detail was seeded in the character profiles and pays off here without being telegraphed. The grief arriving "four months late" is psychologically true. The black crumbs falling into the sink is concrete and right. "She caught herself listening" — this is the chapter's best moment. The body's residual hope, the lean toward the wall, the old reflex — and then nothing. "The conversation, whatever it had been, was over, and had never once been hers." This is the story's final statement and it lands with genuine weight. The ending — eating toast standing up, going out to scrape the slipway out of habit — is exactly the "deliberately small and off-kilter" ending the plan called for. It refuses the tableau, refuses the meaningful final image, and in doing so achieves something more meaningful. **Weaknesses and criticisms:** The chapter is almost entirely summary and reflection rather than scene. This is a structural choice that works thematically but creates some distance. We're told about Dot going "smaller, quieter, the certainty gone out of her like air out of a thing" — this is good, but it's reported rather than shown. The plan called for Dot's tragedy to be rendered more fully in Chapter 7; here it's a brief mention, which is appropriate for a final chapter, but the simile "like air out of a thing" is slightly weak — "a thing" is vague where specificity would serve better. "The plain old horizon she'd stopped being able to see and now could not stop seeing" — this is a good observation but the rhythm is slightly clunky with the repetition of "seeing." The paragraph about Garf staying, with his dogs and his punt and his fence, is good but "his half of a feud now with no one to feud against" is a touch too neat, too aphoristic. It's the kind of line that calls attention to its own cleverness. "The country reaching out a hand into the bays now that the thing was over, now that it was safe to remember the small dying places existed" — this is the chapter's most overtly political/thematic statement and it's slightly on-the-nose compared to the rest of the prose's restraint. The chapter has essentially no dialogue, which is appropriate for a final chapter but means the "Weak Dialogue" metric is largely inapplicable. The plan called for this kind of ending. **Faithfulness to plan:** The chapter follows the plan very closely — the structure's quiet departure, the fish not returning, Maren staying for ordinary reasons, the toast scene, catching herself listening, the final act of scraping the slipway. The plan is executed with skill and without the tidiness the author was trying to avoid. **Overall:** This is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction. It achieves its stated ambitions with consistency and craft. The prose is controlled, the emotional beats are earned, and the refusal of consolation is maintained to the end. Minor weaknesses in a few phrases and the summary-heavy structure keep it from perfection, but it's a strong, serious piece of work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of speculative fiction that demonstrates genuine literary craft throughout its eight chapters. Let me examine it carefully across all dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose voice is remarkably consistent and controlled. The narrator's perspective—a marine biologist reduced to shovelling dead fish while caring for a dementing father—is rendered with specificity and restraint. The detail of Maren counting fish involuntarily ("her mouth did it on its own") is the kind of precise psychological observation that separates competent writing from genuinely good writing. The piece earns its emotional weight through accumulation of concrete detail rather than declaration. The central conceit—that the megastructure is an ear pressed to the planet, listening to an ocean that is spending its fish-bodies as syllables to speak—is genuinely original and intellectually coherent. More importantly, it's deployed with appropriate uncertainty. The story never confirms Pradeep's theory; it holds the interpretive ambiguity honestly. This is sophisticated handling of the unknowable-alien trope. Character work is strong. Maren is fully realized: her fury at her father held alongside her tenderness, her scientific reflex warring with her need for meaning, her refusal to let herself be comforted by the beautiful story. Eli's death scene—the toast complaint as his final words—is genuinely moving and avoids every sentimental trap. Dot's arc from fervent believer to someone saying the words anyway "for form, for the living" is beautifully observed. Garf's shouting at the light ("answer me, you bastard") is the most human moment in the piece. The structural decision to make the alien contact fundamentally indifferent—not hostile, not benevolent, simply not about us—is the story's most daring and successful choice. The Marsh boy rowing into the middle of the most enormous tenderness and being "utterly, perfectly alone in it" is devastating and earned. The pacing across eight chapters is well-managed. The story builds from mundane horror (the smell, the fish) through escalating strangeness to the climactic night of light and sound, then descends into the quiet aftermath. The final chapter's return to toast—burnt-then-scraped, his mother's way—closes the emotional loop with the father's death without being schematic about it. WEAKNESSES: The prose occasionally tips into self-conscious lyricism that slightly undermines its own restraint. Phrases like "the size of an argument you couldn't win" and "the grey to the colour of nothing" are strong, but there are moments where the metaphor-density becomes slightly effortful—"the way grief was present, the way the sickness in the house was present" is one beat too many in a sentence that would land harder with one comparison. This is a minor complaint; the prose is generally excellent. The dialogue is sparse but functional—this is appropriate for the story's register, though Eli's lines do most of the heavy lifting and the other characters' speech is somewhat thin. Sharon the health worker is efficiently sketched but feels slightly like a plot device (delivering the "there's no official left" information) more than a fully inhabited character. The Pradeep email exchange, while thematically necessary, is the weakest section structurally—it's the one place where the story tells us its own interpretation rather than letting us arrive there. The "what if it's not a population, what if it's an individual" idea is given too explicitly, even if the story then wisely refuses to confirm it. The metaphor "the size of an argument you couldn't win" is genuinely excellent. But "indifferent as a mountain" is a cliché. The piece has a few of these—"cold coming up through her boots," "the world swam"—that are minor blemishes on otherwise strong prose. The story is faithful to the prompt, coherent throughout, and the darkness is consistently well-earned. The alien contact is genuinely creative in its conception. The emotional arc—grief for the father, grief for the world, grief for meaning itself—is handled with real maturity. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 17 World Building: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 84.1/100
+ Darkly Comic British YA Fiction — Midnight Blood (Chapter Avg: 85.0 | Final: 86.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **The Prompt Itself: 🩸🩸🩸** Three blood drops at midnight. The beauty of this is its ambiguity. What could it mean? - A genuine emergency — someone hurt, self-harm, an accident - A dark prank or initiation game - A coded message between friends with private meaning - A period emergency (very funny, very British, very YA — could subvert expectations) - A threat / horror element - A drunken text, misread - Part of a treasure hunt or dare cycle - A code from a group chat in-joke gone wrong The darkly comic angle suggests we should NOT play this straight as horror. The best darkly comic stories take something genuinely alarming and reveal it to be mundane — or take something mundane and reveal it to be alarming. The comedy comes from teenage overreaction, social anxiety, the gap between perception and reality. **Possible Protagonists:** - Anxious overthinker who catastrophises everything. Good for comedy. - A neurotic girl, maybe 16-17, who reads the text and spirals. - A boy who's secretly in love with the sender and uses this as an excuse to go to her. - Twins, where one gets the text meant for the other. - A friend group dynamic — the "sensible one" of the group. **Possible Senders:** - Best friend Nina/Priya/Cass who is dramatic and unreliable. - A frenemy. - An ex. - A sibling. - Wrong number entirely (good twist). **British YA flavour:** - Setting: a grim northern town, a sink estate, a posh-but-rotting boarding school, suburban Surrey, Welsh valleys, a seaside town off-season (Skegness, Margate). Off-season seaside is darkly comic gold. - Texture: chip shops, night buses that don't come, corner shops, the specific misery of a wet British night, parents who are useless, NHS waiting rooms, the dread of waking your mum. - Voice: dry, self-deprecating, sweary, observational. Think Sue Townsend, Phoebe Waller-Bridge, Kevin Brooks, Holly Bourne, Malorie Blackman's grit. **Plot Directions:** *Direction A — The Quest Through the Night* Protagonist receives the text, can't reach the sender, sets out across town at midnight to find them. Episodic encounters — odd characters, near-disasters, comic misfortune. Builds dread. Climax reveals the truth. Picaresque structure suits 8 chapters well. *Direction B — The Group Chat Mystery* The text goes to a group. Everyone interprets it differently. We rotate or follow one as they investigate. Reveals secrets within the friendship group. More contained. *Direction C — The Escalation* The three drops are the first of a series. Each chapter, another cryptic message. A game/dare that gets darker. Possibly someone manipulating the protagonist. Could end genuinely sinister or be revealed as elaborate teen drama. *Direction D — The Misdirection* We THINK it's a suicide/self-harm crisis (genuine dark stakes), protagonist races to help — and it turns out to be something absurd (a baking disaster, a first period, a fake-tan accident, a dropped lasagne). But then a real darkness sits underneath — the friend WAS struggling, just not tonight. Tonally rich. *Direction E — The Wrong Number* Text from unknown number. Protagonist responds, gets pulled into a stranger's crisis. Anonymous intimacy. Could be sweet, could be sinister. **Tone calibration ideas:** - The comedy must come from voice and the absurdity of the journey, not from mocking real pain. - Dark = real consequences threatened, death/blood/danger present but often comically deflated. - A strong ending could re-inject genuine emotion after all the comedy — the "earned heart." **Possible Story Beats (Quest version):** 1. The text. The spiral. Decision to go. 2. Sneaking out, first obstacle (parent? night bus?). 3. First weird encounter in town. 4. Reaching sender's house — empty / wrong. 5. A red herring or false lead, deepening dread. 6. Real danger / lowest point. 7. The truth revealed (comic deflation + real heart). 8. Aftermath, resolution, return. **Recurring motifs:** blood (real and not), the colour red, phones dying, the unreliability of communication, mums, chips. # Reflection The strongest engine here is the tension between dread and absurdity — that's where "darkly comic" lives. A straight horror reading wastes the comic potential; a pure farce wastes the dread. The sweet spot is Direction D layered onto Direction A: a **quest through a grim British night**, driven by genuine fear, that keeps deflating into absurdity, but ultimately lands on something true and quietly devastating. What works: - **The quest structure** gives me natural chapter breaks and rising stakes across 8 chapters of ~1000 words. Each chapter = one obstacle/encounter. This is the right shape for the scope. - **An anxious, funny first-person narrator** is the most reliable comedy delivery system and creates instant intimacy. The gap between her catastrophising and reality fuels the humour. - **Off-season seaside or a grim small town** gives me texture, isolation (why can't anyone help?), and that specifically British melancholy-comedy. - **The misdirection ending** (Direction D) prevents the story being either too bleak or too silly. If I reveal the emoji meant something absurd BUT that the friend is genuinely going through something — that gives the heart. The blood-drops should have an explanation that is funny on the surface and sad underneath. Best candidate: it's NOT life-threatening, but it exposes a real crisis the friend has been hiding (e.g., she's been cutting and tonight wasn't that — or she's pregnant — or a domestic situation — or she just couldn't cope and the dramatic text was a cry for the attention nobody gives her). What doesn't work / risks: - **Pure wrong-number (E)** loses the friendship payoff and emotional grounding. I'd rather use a known sender for the heart. - **Group chat (B)** spreads focus too thin for 8 short chapters; better to keep one driving relationship. - **Endless escalation (C)** risks feeling gimmicky and hard to resolve satisfyingly in the word count. - Making the reveal *purely* a joke (e.g., just a spilled curry) would feel cheap and tonally hollow — the darkness needs to be real, even if delivered wryly. - I must avoid trivialising self-harm/mental health while still being funny. The comedy should target the narrator's anxiety, the indifferent world, the absurd obstacles — never the friend's actual pain. Handle the genuine darkness with care and let the ending breathe. Leaning toward: A 16-year-old girl (anxious, sardonic narrator — name like **Lainey** or **Hazel**) gets 🩸🩸🩸 from her best friend **Cass**, the dramatic one everyone's stopped taking seriously. Phone calls fail. She sneaks out into a rain-soaked dead seaside town and crosses it through a series of darkly funny encounters, convinced Cass is dying. The reveal undercuts the literal emergency but uncovers the real one — Cass's loneliness/home situation/something she's been hiding — landing the emotional gut-punch after seven chapters of comedy. The motif of blood (and people not believing girls when they say they're bleeding) can thread through. This balances the prompt's demands: genuinely dark, genuinely funny, distinctly British, properly YA, and structurally suited to eight ~1000-word chapters.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a darkly comic British YA short story across eight chapters of roughly 1000 words each, structured as a quest through a single rain-soaked night. The engine of the piece is the tension between dread and absurdity — genuine fear constantly deflating into the mundane and ridiculous, before the ending re-injects real, earned emotion. **Plot.** Sixteen-year-old narrator **Hazel** receives 🩸🩸🩸 at midnight from her best friend **Cass** — the dramatic one nobody takes seriously anymore. Phone calls go unanswered. Convinced Cass is dying, Hazel sneaks out and crosses their dead, off-season seaside town (a Margate/Skegness-adjacent place I'll call **Sandhaven**), enduring a series of comic obstacles and odd encounters that keep raising the stakes. The reveal will undercut the literal emergency (it was never the life-or-death thing Hazel imagined) while uncovering a real, quieter crisis Cass has been hiding — loneliness, a neglectful home, and a small genuine wound that nobody bothered to ask about. The comedy targets Hazel's catastrophising, the indifferent adult world, and the farce of the journey — never Cass's actual pain. **Voice.** First-person, present-or-past close past tense, sardonic and self-deprecating, fast and observational. Hazel narrates her own anxiety with deadpan wit. British texture throughout: night buses, chippies, corner shops, useless parents, the specific misery of wet pavements and dead seaside towns. Swearing used naturally but not gratuitously. **Stylistic aims.** Short punchy paragraphs; comic similes; the recurring motif of blood and of girls not being believed when they say they're bleeding/hurting; phones dying and failing as a thread; the colour red. Each chapter is one beat/obstacle, ending on a hook or a deflation. **What I'm avoiding.** Straight horror; pure farce with no heart; a cheap joke-only reveal; trivialising self-harm or mental health (the darkness must be real and handled with care); spreading focus across too many POVs. I want the last two chapters to land an emotional gut-punch that recontextualises the comedy, so the whole thing earns its heart rather than just being clever. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Text.** Midnight. Hazel in bed, scrolling, when 🩸🩸🩸 arrives from Cass. Establish their friendship: Cass the drama queen who "cried wolf" over everything (the time she texted DYING and had a verruca). Hazel's anxious spiral, the calls that ring out, the read-receipt that appears then nothing. Establish Hazel's home (single mum on a night shift, useless), the dead town outside. She talks herself out of it, then back into it. Decision: she's going. End on her pulling on trainers. **Chapter 2 — Out.** The escape. Comic obstacle of sneaking out — the back door, the neighbour's security light, a fox, treading in something. Establishes Sandhaven: shuttered arcades, the smell of cold chip fat, rain. Hazel tries calling again; phone at 40% and dropping. A flashback woven in: the last time she saw Cass, a small unexplained argument or distance between them — seeds of guilt. End: the night bus stop, and the bus pulling away without stopping. **Chapter 3 — The First Encounter.** Forced to walk. Meets a darkly comic minor character — e.g. a drunk man, or a lone night-shift kebab-shop worker, or kids on bikes. A funny, slightly menacing exchange that wastes time and ratchets dread. Hazel imagines worst-case Cass scenarios in vivid, ridiculous detail. Maybe she borrows a charger or tries a call that fails. End on a genuinely unsettling note that tips the comedy toward fear. **Chapter 4 — Cass's House.** She reaches Cass's house. It's dark/wrong. Establish Cass's grim home life subtly — the chaos, a parent's car gone, signs nobody's home or nobody cares. Hazel does something stupid and funny to get attention (throws stones, hits a window, wakes the wrong neighbour). The door's locked; no answer. Real fear now. A clue that Cass left — she's not here. End: Hazel finds where she might have gone. **Chapter 5 — False Lead.** Following the clue (the seafront / the pier / an old hideout the two of them shared). Deepening dread mixed with bittersweet memory — this place meant something to them. A red herring: she finds blood (literal — something red), heart stops, but it's deflated (paint, ketchup, a dropped Slush Puppie, a dead pigeon). The relief-then-renewed-fear rhythm. Phone dies here. She's now alone, unreachable, in the dark. End: she hears something. **Chapter 6 — Lowest Point.** The genuine dark heart of the quest. Hazel cold, soaked, lost, phoneless, terrified, possibly in actual minor danger (a sketchy figure, a near-accident, falling, the tide). Strips away the comedy for a moment — real vulnerability, real guilt about how she'd stopped taking Cass seriously, how everyone had. A low, honest interior beat. Then: she spots Cass. **Chapter 7 — The Reveal.** She finds Cass. The literal "emergency" is deflated and faintly absurd (the three blood drops weren't what Hazel feared — a real but minor injury, or a misfired dramatic gesture). The comic relief. BUT underneath: Cass is genuinely not okay — the truth pours out. The neglect, the loneliness, the fact she sent it because Hazel was the only one she thought might come, and half of her didn't believe anyone would. The motif pays off: nobody believes a girl who says she's bleeding. The emotional core lands. They sit in the wreckage of the night together. **Chapter 8 — Aftermath.** The walk home / the dawn. Quieter, warmer. Comedy returns gently (chips at 4am from the one place open, Hazel's mum's furious texts now her phone's charging in the chippy, the absurdity of it all). The friendship recalibrated — Hazel promises she'll always come, and means it. A small, true, hopeful but unsentimental ending. Possibly a final button: a new text arrives, and this time Hazel doesn't hesitate. Close on the image of two girls and the grey sea at dawn.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach This A human writer — particularly one steeped in the British YA tradition this prompt evokes (Kevin Brooks, Melvin Burgess, Holly Bourne, early Sue Townsend, Patrick Ness) — would likely start not from structure but from *voice* and *a specific felt truth*. They'd probably have, somewhere in their own life, the memory of a friend who cried wolf, or the particular cold dread of a midnight text, or the specific texture of their own crap hometown. They'd write toward something they actually believe about teenage friendship, and the plot would be a delivery vehicle for that belief. A human would also be **messier and braver about tone**. Real darkly comic writing earns its darkness by being genuinely uncomfortable, not by gesturing at discomfort and then reassuring you. A human writer might let the reveal sit without fully resolving it. They might leave Hazel implicated — make her partly to blame, make her cowardly in a moment, make her relief curdle into guilt. They'd resist the urge to make everyone learn a lesson. Crucially, a human would be willing to let the comedy be *actually funny* — specific, weird, off-colour, deployed in moments where it's "inappropriate" — rather than tasteful, generic wit. And they'd let the sad bits be *plain* rather than poetic. # Telltale LLM Approaches & Why They'd Hurt This Piece **1. Wrapping everything in a bow.** The single biggest risk here. My plan's Chapter 8 ("friendship recalibrated," "hopeful but unsentimental," "she'll always come, and means it") is dangerously close to a lesson-learned greeting card. A piece about a girl whose dramatic friend is genuinely neglected and lonely should *not* fully resolve. Real neglect doesn't get fixed by one good night. The LLM instinct is to heal the wound; a human leaves it scarred. **2. Writing safely around dark material.** LLMs tend to flinch — to keep the actual darkness offstage, sanitised, or quickly reassured. The reveal "deflates" the literal emergency in my plan, which is good comedically but risks being a *cop-out* if it means the genuinely dark thing (self-harm, neglect, despair) is never allowed to be real and present on the page. A human would be braver about what Cass's wound actually is and refuse to make it tidy. **3. Over-writing / writing to impress.** The "grey sea at dawn" closing image, the "wreckage of the night," the poetic motif management — these can become a pelt of pretty language that flattens the voice. Hazel is a sardonic 16-year-old; if the prose keeps lapsing into lyrical authorial beauty, it stops being *her*. LLMs love a wistful final image. It often reads as imposed. **4. Symmetry and tidiness in structure.** My plan is *very* neat: one obstacle per chapter, deflate-then-fear rhythm, motif planted and paid off. Real life and good fiction are lumpier. The "blood that turns out to be ketchup" red herring (Ch5) is exactly the kind of mechanically satisfying beat an LLM would reach for, and it's a bit of a cliché. Motifs that are too cleanly bookended ("nobody believes a girl who's bleeding" stated thematically) feel engineered. **5. The competent-everything narrator.** LLM protagonists tend to be likeable, perceptive, and basically good — they catastrophise but they're *right* to care, they're brave, they come good. This flattens character. Hazel would be more human if she were also a bit selfish, a bit annoyed at Cass, resentful that Cass always makes everything about herself, *embarrassed* to be out here, half-hoping it's nothing so she can go home and be smug. Mixed, ugly motives. **6. Dialogue that's too articulate.** Teenagers don't speak in clean emotionally-literate paragraphs. The reveal scene risks Cass delivering a coherent monologue about her loneliness. Real version: she'd deflect, joke, lie, contradict herself, refuse to say the thing, get angry at Hazel for coming. **7. Even emotional pacing.** LLMs tend to modulate smoothly. Humans drop sudden tonal trapdoors — a joke mid-tragedy, a horror beat mid-farce — without smoothing the transition. The seams should show. **8. Explaining the joke / explaining the feeling.** LLMs narrate the emotional subtext ("and I realised, in that moment, that I'd never really listened"). A human cuts that line and trusts the reader. **9. Generic Britishness.** "Chip shop, night bus, useless mum" can become a checklist of signifiers rather than a lived-in place. The risk is *cosplaying* British YA. Specificity is the cure: not "a chippy" but the exact name, the exact smell, the woman who works there. # Deep Dive: Where My Plan Risks These Pitfalls - **Ch1's "verruca" gag** is a good instinct but the pattern "dramatic friend cries wolf about trivial thing" is itself a setup that signals the ending too cleanly. Risk: the whole structure becomes a lesson about *believing people*. I should complicate it — maybe Hazel was *right* to be sceptical before; Cass *has* manipulated her; the moral isn't simple. - **Ch5's deflated-blood red herring** — too neat, slightly hacky. Consider cutting or making it stranger/less convenient. - **Ch7's "nobody believes a girl who says she's bleeding" motif payoff** — stating the theme aloud is the death of it. This thematic line should probably *never be said*. It should be felt and left. - **Ch8 chips-at-4am-warm-resolution** — the most LLM beat in the whole plan. The cosy reconciliation, the gentle return of comedy, the hopeful button ("a new text arrives and she doesn't hesitate"). This is a bow. It needs disruption, ambiguity, or a sting. - **The mum being simply "useless"** is lazy. More human: the mum is complicated, or Hazel's relationship with her is the unspoken parallel to Cass's situation, or the mum's absence is more textured than just "night shift." - **Cass as "the dramatic one"** risks being a type. She needs a real, specific, contradictory personality — funny, cruel sometimes, magnetic, exhausting, sharp. # Brainstorm: Ways to Make It More Human **On the ending:** - Let Hazel get there too late for it to be the clean rescue — Cass is *fine* in a way that's almost insulting, or Cass is angry she came, or Cass pretends nothing happened and that's *worse*. - Refuse the reconciliation. End on something unresolved: Hazel walking home alone, not sure if she did a good thing or a stupid thing, the relationship not fixed. - Or: the gut-punch is that this will happen again, and Hazel knows it, and the love is in choosing to come anyway despite knowing nothing changes. That's more honest than "things got better." - A sting button rather than hope: e.g. Hazel finally gets home, exhausted, wrung out — and her phone buzzes with something that recontextualises (Cass already laughing about it in the group chat? Or Hazel realises she's now the one who'll cry wolf and not be believed?). **On the reveal:** - Make the literal cause genuinely funny AND genuinely sad at once — not deflation-then-pivot, but both held in the same image. E.g. the blood is real and trivial and the *reason* is mortifying and the loneliness is underneath all of it simultaneously. - Resist over-explaining Cass's home life. Show one concrete, devastating, specific detail instead of a backstory dump. **On voice:** - Give Hazel petty, real, unflattering thoughts. Let her be irritated. Let her think about whether her trainers are getting ruined. Let her be a coward at one point. - Let her be *funny on purpose* as a defence mechanism, and let that crack. **On specificity:** - Name everything. The chippy is "Mr Cod." The bus is the 34. The hideout is under the pier where someone wrote something specific. Real brand names, real textures. - One running specific gag (the thing Hazel trod in; a specific song; a recurring stranger) rather than thematic motif management. **On structure:** - Allow one chapter to break the obstacle-per-chapter rhythm — collapse two, or let one be almost still and internal. - Cut or transform the convenient red herring. **On the parallel:** - Quietly mirror Hazel's mum and Cass's situation so the theme lives structurally rather than being stated — Hazel half-recognises something of her own life in Cass's, and doesn't fully articulate it. **On the dark material:** - Decide what Cass's wound actually *is* and commit to it being real, present, and not tidied. Whatever it is (self-harm, a domestic incident, a pregnancy scare, sheer despair), it should cost something and not be resolved by dawn.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a darkly comic British YA short story in eight ~1000-word chapters: a single rain-soaked night across a dead off-season seaside town (Sandhaven), narrated by sixteen-year-old **Hazel**, who gets 🩸🩸🩸 at midnight from her best friend **Cass** and goes to find her. **The core truth.** This is about the exhausting, real love of being someone's only person — and the unfairness of that. Cass is dramatic, draining, sometimes manipulative; Hazel is *right* to be sceptical, and goes anyway. The piece refuses the easy moral ("always believe people"). The harder truth: sometimes you come because no one else will, knowing nothing will change, and that's the love. I want the ending unresolved and a little bleak, with the comedy curdling rather than comforting. **The reveal.** The literal cause of the blood will be funny *and* sad held in the same breath — small, mortifying, real — while underneath sits something genuinely dark (Cass's neglect, her loneliness, the fact she half-expected no one to come). I will not state the theme aloud. I'll show one concrete devastating detail rather than a backstory dump. Cass will deflect, lie, joke, and get angry rather than deliver a tidy confession. **Voice & style.** Close first-person past tense, sardonic, fast, self-deprecating. Hazel is funny on purpose as armour, and petty, cowardly, and selfish in flashes — irritated about her trainers while terrified about her friend. Short punchy paragraphs. Comedy deployed at "inappropriate" moments; tonal trapdoors left unsmoothed. Specificity over signifiers: Mr Cod, the 34 bus, named streets, real textures. A quiet structural parallel between Hazel's mum and Cass's home, never explained. **What I'm avoiding.** Bows and reconciliations; lyrical authorial "grey sea at dawn" closings that aren't in Hazel's voice; the convenient ketchup-blood red herring; spoken thematic statements; over-articulate teen dialogue; the competent good-hearted narrator; checklist Britishness; explaining feelings the reader can infer. The darkness must stay real and untidied. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — 🩸🩸🩸.** Midnight, Hazel's room. The text lands. Establish the friendship through Hazel's *irritation* first, not fear — the time Cass texted "I literally can't breathe" over a boy, the verruca, the way Cass makes everything a crisis. Hazel's calls ring out. The read-receipt. She argues herself out of it (it's *Cass*, it's nothing, she's not ruining her sleep again) — and that pettiness is the point. Then a second buzz: nothing, just the read-receipt vanishing. Something tips. She gets up, hating herself for it. End: lacing trainers in the dark, furious. **Chapter 2 — The 34.** Sneaking out. Mum's not home — a half-detail about where Mum is that Hazel skates past too quickly (the parallel, planted). Comic escape: the back gate, the neighbour's sensor light, treading in something she'll mention all night. Sandhaven established with specifics — shuttered Mr Whippy, the smell of cold fat off Mr Cod. Phone at 38%. She runs for the 34; it doesn't stop. Woven in: a recent coolness between her and Cass — Hazel pulled away lately, and feels it now. End: walking, alone, rain starting. **Chapter 3 — Night People.** The long walk. A darkly comic encounter — a bloke at the bus shelter, or lads on bikes circling, menace and absurdity at once, wasting time, frightening her a little. Hazel's catastrophising runs vivid and ridiculous and then suddenly *not* funny. She tries Cass again; voicemail, the chirpy outgoing message that now sounds awful. Phone dropping fast. End on a real chill — something in the encounter she can't laugh off. **Chapter 4 — Number 6.** Cass's house. Dark, wrong, a car gone. One devastating concrete detail of the home life shown not told (bins, a note, the state of the front room glimpsed through glass). Hazel does something stupid and funny to rouse her — wakes the wrong neighbour, who is furious and unhelpful. No Cass. A clue she's gone out. End: Hazel knows the one place, and dreads it. **Chapter 5 — Under the Pier.** Heading to their old place — under the pier, where they used to go, freighted with bittersweet shared history rendered in one specific memory, not nostalgia-soup. The walk gives Hazel space to land an honest, unflattering interior beat: that she's been *leaving* Cass, growing out of her, and this — tonight — is the guilt of it. No ketchup-blood gag. Instead a genuine wrong-footing: she finds Cass's phone, dropped, screen cracked, lit up with Hazel's own missed calls. End: but no Cass, and the tide's in. **Chapter 6 — Cold.** Lowest point, comedy stripped back. Hazel's phone dies here. Soaked, frightened, calling Cass's name into the dark, half-convinced of the worst, genuinely vulnerable — a near-miss with the water or a slip on the rocks. Real fear, real guilt, plain language. No lyricism. Then a small human sound — and she finds Cass, sitting where she shouldn't be, alive, weirdly calm. **Chapter 7 — Three Drops.** The reveal, held in one breath: funny and devastating. The literal blood is real, minor, mortifying — and Cass is furious Hazel came / pretends it's nothing / lies / deflects with a joke, then cracks sideways into the actual thing without ever naming it cleanly. The genuine dark underneath surfaces through what she *won't* say and one concrete detail. The theme lives in the gap. Hazel realises she was right not to believe and came anyway — and that this will happen again. No catharsis-hug; something more uncomfortable and true. They sit. **Chapter 8 — Mr Cod.** Pre-dawn. They walk back; the only place open is the chippy. Comedy returns but *curdled* — they laugh, and it's not really better. Hazel's phone charges enough to show Mum's texts (the parallel closes quietly, unspoken). A small honest exchange that fixes nothing. The sting button: not hope, but a clear-eyed acceptance — Hazel knows she'll come next time too, and knows that's both love and a trap. Final beat undercut, not lyrical: something small, deadpan, and bleakly funny that leaves the wound open. End.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Hazel Okonkwo-Price **Background.** Sixteen, lives on Sandhaven's edge in a pebble-dashed semi on Wykeham Road, the kind of street where the streetlight outside hers has been flickering for three years and the council keep sending letters about the wrong house. Mum is a care assistant doing nights at Greenways, the dementia home up the hill — gone four evenings out of seven, which Hazel has stopped having feelings about, or thinks she has. Dad's in Coventry with someone called Dawn; sends money, not much else. Hazel is the sensible one by default rather than nature — there was no one else to be it. She does her homework not out of ambition but because the structure holds her together. She wants, vaguely, to get *out* — not anywhere specific, just out — and feels guilty about wanting it. **Personality & flaws.** Hazel is funny as a defence mechanism and weaponises it; she'd rather get a laugh than be understood. She catastrophises in vivid, almost loving detail — she'll have Cass dead, bled out, on a slab, and her own black outfit chosen for the funeral, before she's even off the sofa. She is also, importantly, *petty and a bit of a coward*. Her first feeling on getting the text is annoyance, not fear. She's been quietly editing Cass out of her life lately — taking longer to reply, sitting with the netball girls at lunch — and she knows it, and it sits in her like a swallowed stone. She trod in something at the start of the night and will not stop bringing it up, because complaining about her trainers is easier than thinking about what she's walking towards. **Idiosyncrasies.** Bites the skin round her thumbnail when scared, then is disgusted with herself. Narrates her own life like she's already telling someone the story of it later. Has a thing about even numbers. Pretends not to know the words to songs she knows every word to. **Physicality.** Tall, slightly hunched as if apologising for the height, mixed-race, hair she keeps under a beanie because dealing with it at midnight is not happening. Moves fast and graceless. Resting face that teachers read as cheek. Dressed for the night in joggers, her mum's too-big coat that smells of Greenways, and the ruined trainers. **Motives & relationships.** She loves Cass and is exhausted by Cass and these are the same feeling. She goes tonight not out of pure goodness but out of a tangle: guilt, fear, love, and the dim awareness that if she doesn't, no one will — and that's a thing she knows about herself too. Her relationship with her mum is the unspoken twin of all this: two people orbiting each other's absences. **Voice.** > "Three blood drops. Not even a word. Cass once sent me a forty-second voice note crying because Greggs ran out of the cheese-and-onion bakes, so forgive me if I didn't immediately phone an ambulance." > *Walk faster. No — that's worrying, walking faster means I think it's real. Walk normal. Walk like a person whose best mate isn't currently dead in a ditch wearing the top I lent her and never got back.* # Cass Brennan **Background.** Sixteen, lives at 6 Marine Crescent, a terrace two streets back from the front, with her mum Donna and a rotating cast of Donna's boyfriends, currently a bloke called Lee whose presence Cass measures by the number of cans in the recycling. Donna works the bar at the Anchor and "has her own stuff going on," a phrase Cass uses like a shield. There's an older brother, Jordan, who got out — joined the army, sends Cass £20 on her birthday and nothing else. Cass has been raising herself in the gaps for years and has decided to find this glamorous rather than tragic, which is its own kind of armour. **Personality & flaws.** Cass is magnetic and exhausting and cruel in small accurate ways when she's hurt. She turns everything into a performance because performance is the only reliable way she's found to make people look at her — and she's correct that it works, and it's also why no one believes her anymore, including, lately, Hazel. She cried wolf because the wolf was real and no one came for the actual wolf, so she started inventing smaller ones just to test the alarm. She's smarter than she lets on, funnier than Hazel, and will sabotage a good moment because she can't sit inside being cared for; it itches. **Idiosyncrasies.** Always cold, always nicking other people's hoodies (hence the borrowed top). Names things — her phone is "Geoffrey." Laughs hardest at the worst moments. Picks at a thing on her arm she won't let you see. **Physicality.** Small, sharp, all elbows and eyeliner, hair dyed a red that's grown out at the roots. Sits where she shouldn't — on the harbour wall, the wrong side of a railing — because attention is the point. Talks with her whole face. Goes very still and very flat-voiced when it's actually bad, which Hazel has learned to fear more than the drama. **Motives & relationships.** She sent the text to Hazel and only Hazel, and half of her sent it as a test: *will you still come?* She loves Hazel and has clocked Hazel pulling away and is too proud to say so, so she made a crisis instead, the only language she trusts. She'd rather be furious that Hazel came than admit she needed her to. **Voice.** > "Oh my GOD, don't look at me like that. I'm not on a ledge, Hazel, I'm sitting on a wall. There's a difference. The difference is about four feet and you ruining your night for nothing." > "It's fine. It's genuinely fine. People bleed. You bleed, like, all the time. Nobody comes running with a torch and their mum's coat looking like a missing-persons poster, do they. Anyway. Did you bring chips." # Donna Brennan (Cass's mum) **Background.** Late thirties, looks older in a hard light and younger in a kind one. Had Jordan at nineteen, Cass at twenty-three, has been managing ever since with the volume turned up. Pulls pints at the Anchor, knows everyone, is liked, and that being-liked is something she needs the way Cass does — the apple didn't fall far. Not a villain. Just overwhelmed, self-absorbed by necessity, and genuinely unaware of how often the house is empty around her daughter, because she's telling herself a story where Cass is "so independent, that one." **Personality & flaws.** Warm in bursts, absent in stretches. Means well at a distance. Will buy Cass something expensive and forget her birthday in the same fortnight. Treats Cass's dramatics as proof Cass is "fine and attention-seeking" — which lets Donna off the hook, which is the convenient part. **Physicality.** Bottle-blonde, gym leggings, a laugh you hear across a car park, a way of touching your arm when she talks to you that makes you feel chosen for exactly as long as the conversation lasts. **Motives & relationships.** Loves Cass in a way that's real and insufficient. Will appear mostly through her absence and one glimpsed detail. **Voice.** > "She's a drama, that one, always has been — out of the womb performing. Honestly, Hazel love, don't let her wind you up. She'll have forgotten all about it by morning." # Hazel's Mum — Grace Price **Background.** Appears only by text and through Hazel's avoidance. Care assistant, kind to other people's parents on nights, tired down to the bone, doing her sincere best. The structural twin to Donna without the carelessness — Grace's absence is *for* Hazel (the wages) and that's precisely what makes it harder to be angry about, so Hazel isn't, which costs her something. **Voice (texts).** > *Working late love theres pasta in the fridge dont stay up. Lock the back x* > *Hazel why is your phone off. Hazel. Ring me. HAZEL.*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — 🩸🩸🩸 The text came just after midnight. 🩸🩸🩸 Three blood drops. Not even a word. Not even a *full stop*, which, fine, no one punctuates a text, but you'd think if you were actually dying you might at least attempt a vowel. I was on the sofa, which I want it on record was not where I should have been. I should have been in bed. It was a Tuesday. I had a maths assessment second period that I had not revised for because revising for it felt like admitting it existed. Instead I was watching a video of a man restoring a rusted spanner, which I cannot explain and won't try to, and eating Coco Pops dry out of the box because the milk had gone and I couldn't be bothered to write *milk* on the whiteboard on the fridge, which is the only system holding our house together. So I was awake. Which is the thing I keep coming back to. If I'd been asleep I'd never have seen it till morning, and morning is a different country. In the morning a text like that is something you mention at the bus stop. *Cass did the bleeding emoji thing again, you should've seen me, I nearly didn't even reply.* In the morning it's a story you tell. At midnight it just sits on the screen and pulses. I looked at it for a long time. Long enough that the man finished the spanner and started on a kettle. Here is what you need to understand about Cass Brennan before you judge me, and you will judge me, I can feel it. In February she sent me a forty-second voice note in which she was crying — properly, snot-crying, the real ugly thing — because the Greggs by the bus station had run out of cheese-and-onion bakes. In March she texted *I literally can't breathe* and I phoned her in a state and she'd seen Dylan Frame holding hands with Megan Pyatt by the arcades. In year nine she texted *DYING* in capitals and when I sprinted round to Marine Crescent she had a verruca. A verruca. She showed it to me like it was a war wound. She wanted me to *look* at it. So you can see how three drops of blood with no words might land slightly differently on me than it would on, say, a normal person who hadn't been trained for two years to treat Cass Brennan's emergencies the way you treat a car alarm in a car park. Which is: it's going off, yes, technically, but no one's stealing the car. They never are. I texted back. *what.* The two ticks went blue. Read. Then nothing. I watched the little space under the message where it tells you she's typing. It didn't say she was typing. It didn't say anything. It just had the time and the word *Read* and underneath it the three drops, redder somehow now I was looking properly, like the longer you stare at red the more red it gets. *cass.* I sent. *not funny. what.* Read. Nothing. And here's the bit I'm not proud of. The bit that, if I'm being a person about this and not just telling you the version where I come out alright, I have to put in. I was annoyed. Not scared. Annoyed. Because I knew this game, I'd played this game, and the game was: Cass does something dramatic, you panic, she gets to be the centre of the panic for an evening, and at the end of it there's a verruca or a bake-shortage and you've lost a night's sleep and she's *fine*, she's better than fine, she's been topped up. That's what it was for. She ran the engine off your worry. And lately — and this is the swallowed-stone part, this is the bit I'd been not-looking-at the way I don't look at the whiteboard — lately I'd stopped wanting to be the petrol. I'd been slow to reply to her. Sat with the netball lot at lunch twice. Left her on read for a whole day in a way I told myself was an accident and absolutely was not. I was *editing her out*. Quietly, the way you turn a dimmer down so slow no one notices the room going dark, including you. So when the blood drops came I thought, with a meanness that I can still taste: *not tonight, Cass. I'm not doing it tonight. I've got maths. You can be alive in the morning and tell me about your verruca then.* I put the phone face down on the sofa arm. I ate four more Coco Pops. I picked the phone back up. Read. Nothing. And the thing was — the thing that wouldn't sit right — Cass doesn't *do* nothing. That's the whole point of Cass. After the bakes she'd voice-noted me eleven more times. After Dylan and Megan she'd phoned. The drama is never the end of it; the drama is the *invitation*. You're supposed to come running so she can perform you the rest. The silence wasn't part of the act. The silence was wrong, and I knew it was wrong the way you know a noise in your own house at night, somewhere underneath the part of you that's still trying to argue you're imagining it. I rang her. It rang out. Six rings — I counted, I count things — and then her voice, bright and stupid and recorded weeks ago: *It's Cass, obviously, you've got Geoffrey, leave a thing.* Beep. I hung up. Rang again. Out. I sat there with the cold box of cereal in my lap and the streetlight outside doing its broken flicker through the curtains, on, off, on, and I thought about the top I'd lent her, the green one, that she'd never given back, and how I'd pictured it on her in a coffin before I'd even decided to move, that's how fast my brain goes, that's the catastrophe machine, always two steps ahead with the funeral planned. Mum was at work. Greenways. Wouldn't be back till seven. I bit the skin round my thumbnail and hated myself for it. Then I got up, in the dark, furious, and felt around the bottom of the stairs for my trainers.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished opening chapter that demonstrates genuine command of voice, tone, and character. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The first-person narration is exceptionally well-realised. Hazel's voice is consistent, sardonic, and genuinely funny without being try-hard. The comedy lands because it's grounded in specificity — the rusted spanner video, the dry Coco Pops, the whiteboard system. The self-awareness ("the bit I'm not proud of") is deployed with precision, and the narrator's unreliability is established without being announced. The "dimmer switch" metaphor for editing Cass out is earned and precise rather than decorative. The catastrophe machine — planning the funeral before deciding to move — is both funny and revealing of character. EXECUTION OF THE PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely and well. The irritation-before-fear structure is executed exactly as intended. The Cass backstory is delivered through specific, comic examples (verruca, cheese-and-onion bakes, Dylan Frame) rather than summary. The pettiness is present and pointed. The "swallowed stone" of Hazel pulling away is planted. The silence being "wrong" is the tipping point, and it's handled with restraint. The chapter ends with lacing trainers in the dark, furious — precisely as planned. SPECIFICITY AND TEXTURE: The details are excellent throughout. "Geoffrey" is planted via the voicemail. The flickering streetlight. The whiteboard. The six rings counted. These are the details of a writer who understands that specificity is characterisation. TONAL CONTROL: The tonal trapdoors are handled well. The comedy of the verruca and the bakes gives way to the genuine wrongness of the silence, and the transition is managed without announcement. The line "the silence wasn't part of the act" is the pivot, and it works. MINOR CRITICISMS: The "two steps ahead with the funeral planned" passage is slightly over-explained — the image of the green top in the coffin is strong, but "that's how fast my brain goes, that's the catastrophe machine, always two steps ahead with the funeral planned" tells us what we've just been shown. This is a small instance of tell-after-show. The "swallowed stone" metaphor, while functional, is the one moment where the prose reaches for something slightly more literary than Hazel's voice strictly warrants. The chapter is also almost entirely setup — which is appropriate for Chapter 1, but means the "compelling plot" dimension is necessarily limited at this stage. DIALOGUE: There is no spoken dialogue in this chapter, only texts, which is appropriate. The texted exchanges are perfectly calibrated. DARKNESS AND COMEDY: The balance is exactly right. The comedy doesn't undercut the genuine unease; it coexists with it in the way the plan intended. OVERALL: This is a high-quality piece of YA fiction that demonstrates genuine craft. The voice is distinctive and consistent, the character work is nuanced, and the tonal control is impressive. It avoids almost all the pitfalls the plan identified. The few weaknesses are minor. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — The 34 The trainers were where I'd kicked them off, which is to say in the exact place engineered to do maximum damage to a person in a hurry. I got the left one on standing up and hopped into the kitchen, and on the way I knocked the whiteboard pen off the fridge and didn't pick it up, and the note Mum had left was still there from teatime in her round careful capitals: *WORKING LATE LOVE THERES PASTA IN THE FRIDGE DONT STAY UP. LOCK THE BACK X.* There was no pasta. There hadn't been pasta. I think she'd meant to do pasta and then had to leave, and writing the note was the bit she could manage, so she'd managed it, and gone. I didn't think about this. I want to be clear that I specifically did not think about it. I read it for one second and put my other trainer on and decided not to. I didn't take the front. The front door sticks and announces itself, a great gunshot crack you can hear from the bottom of the street, and even though there was no one home to hear it I went out the back on instinct, the way you tiptoe in an empty house. The back gate was the next problem. We don't use the back gate. The bolt had rusted into a sort of decision it had made years ago and would not be talked out of, and I stood there in my mum's coat working at it with both thumbs going *come on, come ON,* whispering it, to a gate, at half past midnight, like the gate was being unreasonable. It went. I went through. And the second I was in the alley the Hendersons' security light came on like the eye of God, white and total, and I froze in it the way you freeze, as if standing very still makes you invisible and not, in fact, the single most lit-up object in Sandhaven. A cat looked at me from a wheelie bin. I looked at the cat. Neither of us did anything about it. And then I trod in something. I'm not going to describe it. I want to, because it has been on my mind ever since, a cold wet give underfoot and a smell that came up immediate and personal, but I'm not going to, because if I start I'll never stop, and the night was already going to be long enough without me dedicating it to the thing on my trainer. I'll just say I knew straight away it was the kind of thing you don't get off. The kind you have to make peace with. I made a sound I'd be embarrassed to write down and kept moving, scraping the foot along the kerb every few steps like a horse with an opinion. Out of the alley and onto the front and the town just — opened up dead in front of me. Sandhaven at night, off-season, is a town somebody's switched off and forgotten to take home. The arcades were down behind their shutters, the cartoon faces on the fronts grinning at nobody, BINGO and 2P FALLS and the Mr Whippy painted twelve foot high holding a cornet, all of it dark. The tide was a long way out. You could hear it but you couldn't see it, just a black flatness past the railings and that smell — wet rope, weed, salt, and underneath everything, all the way along the parade, cold chip fat, because Mr Cod's deep-fat fryers had been running every night for thirty years and the building had given up trying not to smell of them. It started to rain. Of course it did. Not proper rain, the mean kind, fine and sideways, the kind that doesn't look like much and soaks you to the bone in minutes out of spite. 38%, my phone said. I'd looked. I shouldn't have looked, but I'd looked, and now I knew, the way you know the exact mileage you've got left before you're walking. I rang Cass again on the move. Out. *Geoffrey, leave a thing.* I hung up before the beep this time. I couldn't take the brightness of her recorded voice, that stupid happy version of her recorded on some afternoon when everything was fine, telling me to leave a thing while I scraped a thing off my shoe in the rain trying to find the live version, the one that had gone silent. The 34 was the plan. The 34 runs hourly, all night, the only one that does, a ghost of a route with about four passengers across the whole of Kent and one of them usually asleep against the window with his mouth open. The stop's by the war memorial. I got there with my chest going and saw the lights of it already coming down the front, gold and warm and full of nobody, and I put my arm out, properly out, no ambiguity, an arm that meant *stop*. It didn't stop. It came on at the same steady speed, the driver looking dead ahead, and the lit-up empty inside of it slid past me close enough that I could see the grab-rails and the empty seats and the sad little sign about not putting your feet up, and then it was past, its tail-lights going small and red down towards the harbour, and the rain came in behind it and I stood there with my arm still half out like an idiot. Maybe he didn't see me. Maybe the war memorial isn't a stop after midnight, maybe they cut it, maybe — I had a whole conversation with the driver in my head where I explained to him exactly what kind of night I was having and he apologised. None of it brought the bus back. The next 34 was an hour away. Cass had gone silent forty minutes ago, give or take, and I thought about that, forty minutes of nothing from a girl who can't go forty seconds, and the catastrophe machine started up again, and this time I didn't tell it to stop. I put my head down and started walking.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this piece is a strong execution of the plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through the key elements. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Hazel's voice is consistently rendered and matches the profile precisely. The sardonic self-awareness ("whispering it, to a gate, at half past midnight, like the gate was being unreasonable"), the petty fixation on the trainer, the catastrophising that she "didn't tell it to stop" — all of this is character-consistent and feels earned rather than performed. The line "I made a sound I'd be embarrassed to write down" is exactly the kind of self-deprecating deflection the profile calls for. The narrator-as-storyteller quality ("I want to be clear that I specifically did not think about it") is well-deployed. THE PASTA NOTE: This is the chapter's most quietly devastating moment. The plan called for a "half-detail about where Mum is that Hazel skates past too quickly (the parallel, planted)" and the execution is excellent. "I think she'd meant to do pasta and then had to leave, and writing the note was the bit she could manage, so she'd managed it, and gone. I didn't think about this. I want to be clear that I specifically did not think about it." This is show-don't-tell at its best — the reader understands everything Hazel is refusing to understand, and the refusal itself is characterisation. SANDHAVEN: The setting is rendered with the specificity the plan demanded. Mr Whippy, Mr Cod, the arcades, the tide — these aren't generic seaside signifiers but accumulate into a real place. "A town somebody's switched off and forgotten to take home" is a strong image that earns its slight poeticism because it's in Hazel's register, not an authorial intrusion. THE BUS: The missed bus is a perfect small comic-tragic beat. The detail of the arm still half-out "like an idiot," the imagined conversation with the driver — this is the comedy-as-armour the plan specified, and it lands. MINOR WEAKNESSES: The trainer-thing gag is well-set-up but slightly over-milked — "the kind you have to make peace with" is good, but the horse simile ("like a horse with an opinion") is the one moment where the metaphor feels slightly strained rather than natural. It's not a serious failing. The chapter is perhaps slightly light on the "recent coolness between her and Cass" that the plan specified should be woven in — it's gestured at with "the live version, the one that had gone silent" but not quite as developed as planned. DIALOGUE: There is no dialogue in this chapter, which is appropriate — it's a solo journey chapter. The absence is not a weakness. TONAL CONTROL: The chapter manages the tonal trapdoors well. The comedy of the gate, the cat, the trainer, the bus — and then the final paragraph where the catastrophe machine starts and she doesn't stop it. The shift is earned and not over-explained. FOLLOWED PLAN: The chapter hits nearly all its planned beats: sneaking out, Mum's note (planted parallel), the sensor light, treading in something, Sandhaven established, phone at 38%, the 34 not stopping, rain starting, walking alone. The "recent coolness" is slightly underplayed compared to the plan. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled YA literary fiction with a genuine voice. The comedy is specific and character-rooted, the darkness is present without being announced, and the prose never tips into purple territory. A very strong chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — Night People Marine Crescent is twenty minutes from the war memorial if you cut up past the leisure centre and through the bit by the railway. Less if you run, but I wasn't running, because running meant I thought it was real, and walking meant I could still pretend I was just a girl out for a walk who happened to be furious and damp and accompanied by a smell. I did the maths on it the whole way, because doing maths on it was better than the alternative. Forty minutes of silence. No — fifty now. People go quiet for fifty minutes all the time. People go quiet for fifty minutes because their phone's on charge in another room, because they've fallen asleep with the telly on, because the drama wore itself out and they're embarrassed and waiting for you to text first so they can say *omg sorry I'm fine babe* and you can both pretend it never happened. That was the most likely thing. That was, statistically, by miles, the thing. But the brain doesn't do statistics at one in the morning. The brain does the green top. The brain does her on the bathroom floor of number 6 with the door locked and Donna out and Lee's cans in the recycling and no one, no one in that house, no one coming up those stairs but me. The brain builds the whole scene and lights it and then makes you walk towards it whether you want to or not. I cut up by the leisure centre. The pool's been shut for refurbishment for a year and a half; the banner saying SPLASHING BACK SOON had come away at one corner and was slapping itself against the wall in the wind, *thwack, thwack,* and I want to tell you I didn't jump every single time, but I did. There were lads under the bridge by the railway. Three of them, on bikes, doing those slow circles people do on bikes when they've got nowhere to be — not riding, just orbiting, the back wheel ticking. One was on his phone, the light up under his chin. They had hoods up. They saw me before I saw them, which is the worst way round, and I felt my whole body do the thing girls' bodies do, the recalculation, keys-between-fingers except I had no keys, just a dying phone and a coat that smelled of a dementia home. "Alright," one of them said. Not nasty. Just there. "Alright," I said, and kept walking, eyes on the bit of pavement ten feet ahead, the practised not-looking that's somehow also looking, that takes everything in. They didn't say anything else for a bit. The bikes ticked. I walked through the orbit of them and out the other side and I'd nearly relaxed when the one who'd spoken called, conversational, behind me: "You looking for someone?" I stopped. I shouldn't have stopped. Stopping is what you don't do. But there was something in it, the *you looking for someone,* like he knew, like it was a real question and not a chat-up, and I turned round because I'm an idiot and because part of me is always, always hoping the next person will be the one who hands me the answer. "Why," I said. "Have you—" He laughed. They all laughed, a bit, soft, and the wheel ticked, and he said, "Dunno. You just look like you're looking for someone," and that was it. That was the whole thing. He wasn't being kind and he wasn't being cruel, he was just a boy on a bike at one in the morning telling me what my face looked like, and somehow that was worse than either, because it meant I was wearing it, the panic, out loud, where strangers could read it off me in the dark. "No," I said. "Just walking." "Bit cold for it." "Yeah." And I turned and went, fast now, properly fast, and behind me one of them said something I didn't catch and they all laughed again and the laughing followed me up the road, not at me exactly, just out there, in the world, the careless ordinary sound of people who weren't having my night, and it made me feel about nine years old. I got round the corner and out of sight and rang Cass. Six rings. *It's Cass, obviously, you've got Geoffrey, leave a thing.* I'd heard that message four times now and every time it got worse. The brightness of it.
