Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Marlowe ("Mar") **Background & personality.** Mar is sixty-one. Before the world ended she ran a cafe — not a good one, she'd tell you, but a warm one, the kind regulars came to because she remembered their orders and yelled at them affectionately. She has fed people her whole life: a husband (dead before all this, heart), no children of her own, a long string of strays and stragglers and difficult relatives. Feeding is how she loves and how she controls. She would not put it that way. She'd say she just likes a clean kitchen and people who eat what's put in front of them. She is practical to a fault, unsentimental on the surface and a wreck underneath. She does the hard things — the butchering, the rationing, the deciding — because she's decided nobody else should have to, which is partly kindness and partly that she doesn't trust anyone else to do it right. Her flaw is exactly here: the ladle is power, and she likes holding it. When Della won't eat her carrots, some small part of Mar is offended in a way that's bigger than carrots. **Idiosyncrasies.** Tastes everything off the spoon and licks it clean and puts the spoon back in the pot, which drives Eun mad. Talks to the food. Keeps salt like it's currency, doles it grain by grain, has a whole philosophy about it. Hums tunelessly when she cooks and doesn't know she does it. Hates waste with a religious intensity — she'll eat the part nobody wants so it isn't thrown out, and calls this a treat to herself so nobody argues. **Physicality.** Heavyset, gone soft and then gone thin again in the hunger, so her clothes hang now. Bad knees. Red, cracked hands she's stopped noticing. Moves slowly but doesn't stop. When she's frightened she gets very still and very quiet and her hands keep working on their own. She doesn't meet your eye when she's lying; she looks at the pot. **Motives & relationships.** Her whole motive is keeping these three alive, which curdles into keeping them *fed*, which curdles into keeping them *here, with her, content*. Sol is the one — she half-raised them, would do anything, has done worse than she'll admit. Della she loves and is exhausted by. Eun she respects and finds cold; they're the two grown-ups at the table and don't always like it. > "Eat the carrots, Della, I didn't carry them up two flights in the snow for them to look at me." > *I told myself it was just meat. You tell yourself a lot of things over a hot stove. The trick is to keep stirring so your hands have somewhere to be.* --- # Sol **Background & personality.** Sol is seventeen, was eleven when it started, which means they barely remember the before and aren't haunted by it the way the adults are — they don't grieve a world they never really had. This makes them eerily adaptable. What would horrify Della rolls off Sol. They're sweet, watchful, easily delighted by small things — a card game, the burnt edge of anything, snow shapes — and underneath the sweetness is a kid who learned young that the universe doesn't owe you a tomorrow, so you might as well be warm tonight. Mar half-raised them and Sol loves Mar with the unembarrassed totality of a child, which is a terrible weapon and neither of them knows it. Sol's adaptability is what makes them go *toward* the warmth so willingly in the end — they were never as attached to being human as the adults assume. They don't experience the bloom as horror. They experience it as not being cold, and not being alone, and being able to feel Della still there. **Idiosyncrasies.** Wants the burnt bits, the crusts, the parts other people don't — a thing that reads as charming and is, in hindsight, a little ominous. Names things: named the mutants "the slow ones" and it stuck. Hums. Picks up tunes from nowhere. Asks blunt questions adults flinch from ("Did it hurt it, when you—?"). **Physicality.** Tall and unfinished, all wrist, a body that grew faster than the food allowed. A buzzed head Mar keeps cutting. Quick, light, the only one who still moves like there's joy in moving. Sits too close to people, leans on them. After the meals, a warmth and a brightness to them that the others put down to youth. **Motives & relationships.** Sol wants Mar happy and the family whole and the night warm; that's the whole of it. Loyal to Mar above the others. Fond of Della. Wary of Eun's coldness, hurt by it. When things fracture, Sol can't understand why everyone's afraid of feeling *better*. > "It doesn't feel bad, Mar. I keep telling you. It feels like when you fall asleep on someone." > "Della's not gone-gone. I can still—" *They put their hand flat on their own chest, like that explained it. It did, to them.* --- # Della **Background & personality.** Della is in her seventies, was a schoolteacher, has opinions about everything and a low tolerance for being managed. Sharp-tongued, vain in a faded way, difficult in the specific way of someone who used to be in charge of a room and now can't get up the stairs unassisted. She and Mar bicker like sisters. She is *not* a saint and the story will not make her one — she's the one who won't eat the carrots, who criticizes Mar's seasoning, who has a comment. She's dying and knows it — was dying before the meat — and her sharpness is partly fear worn as armor. The food doesn't cure her. It makes her gentle, which is the most frightening thing that could happen to Della, because gentle isn't Della. When she starts smiling at the corner of the room and talking to her dead, that's when Mar should be most afraid, and is. **Idiosyncrasies.** Hoards small comforts — a particular blanket, a chipped cup that's *hers*. Corrects people's grammar even now, even dying. Pretends to dislike Sol and dotes on them shamelessly. Refuses to be fed by hand right up until she doesn't, which is its own kind of surrender. **Physicality.** Birdlike, getting smaller. Beautiful white hair she still tends. Hands that shake. Sits very upright as a matter of pride until the end, when she sinks into the chair like she's finally allowed. The gold comes into her first — under the thin skin of her wrists — and Mar calls it good color. **Motives & relationships.** Wants dignity, warmth, the last word, and not to be a burden — she'd never say the last one. Loves Mar and won't show it cleanly. Adores Sol. Tolerant of Eun. Her real arc is from fighting death to welcoming it, which the food makes terribly easy. > "Your hand's too heavy with the salt and your heart's too heavy with everything else. Sit down. You're hovering." > *Late, near the end:* "Oh, leave the cup, Marlowe. I won't need it where the warm is." --- # Eun **Background & personality.** Eun is in her forties, was a lab tech or a nurse — something procedural, something that taught her to distrust how things *feel* and count what's actually there. She's the one who keeps inventory, who says the unwelcome number out loud. Not cold exactly, but contained, careful, slow to attach because she's lost people and learned the lesson. The group reads her as the skeptic; really she's just the only one who can't be fooled, and that's not virtue — it's a fluke. Because here's the thing the story turns on: Eun eats the meat too, that first night, and it tastes of *nothing*. Just meat, gamey, fine. No childhood, no mother's kitchen, no warm dark. She doesn't have whatever the others have. So the lure can't take her, and she watches the people she lives with go somewhere she can't follow, and it's lonely and infuriating and she handles it badly — not bravely. She leaves because she's afraid and because she's angry that she's the only one who's afraid. **Idiosyncrasies.** Counts. Cans, days, breaths, mutants at the glass. Hates Mar's spoon-licking. Keeps a notebook of observations nobody asked for. Says "we don't actually know that" a lot. When stressed, gets pedantic and brittle rather than loud. **Physicality.** Lean, economical, no wasted movement. Cuts her own hair short and uneven. A way of standing back from things, arms folded, watching. The only one who *doesn't* get the warm flush after the meals, which she notices, which scares her, which nobody else will discuss. **Motives & relationships.** Wants to stay alive and sane and doesn't fully trust that those two things are still compatible here. Closest to Mar — they're the two adults — which is what makes the parting hurt. Frustrated by Sol's calm. Frightened of what Della became. Her departure isn't a triumph; it's a flight, and Mar's failure to follow is the real wound. > "I didn't taste anything. You all keep — it was meat. It was just meat. Why am I the only one who—" *She stops, because nobody's listening, because they're smiling.* > "Come with me, Mar. Please. I'm counting and the number's wrong and I'm the only one who thinks the number's wrong."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Three Cans There were three cans left and I made them last like a magician makes a coin last, which is to say I lied about it. Two were beans and one was something that had lost its label in the damp, and I dented all three open with the church key and tipped them into the big pot and stretched them with snowmelt and the last of the dried thyme that smelled more like dust than thyme anymore. I salted it grain by grain, the way I do, holding each one up like it owed me money. Then I stood over the stove and stirred and hummed without knowing I was humming until Eun said, from over by the window, "You're doing it again," and I stopped. We lived in the greenhouse by then. The big municipal one, all glass and iron ribs, that used to grow tomatoes for the farmer's market and orchids for nobody. Snow had come up the sides of it through the winter so that the lower panes were white and the light that got in came down from the top, gray and clean. It kept the heat better than you'd think, with the stove going and the four of us breathing and the old grow-lamps we'd wired to the one panel that still bothered. It felt, on a good evening, like being inside a held breath. On a bad one it felt like being inside a jar somebody had set on a shelf and forgotten. This was somewhere in between. "Smells like beans," said Della from her chair, in the tone of a woman identifying a smell beneath her. "It's stew," I said. "It's beans you've drowned." "It's stew, Della, and there are carrots in it, and you'll eat them." "There are not carrots in it." There were carrots in it. Two, the soft kind, the kind you cook because the alternative is throwing them out, and I would sooner eat the pot. I'd carried those carrots up the back stairs through the snow eleven days ago and I had been saving them and I was not going to be told there were no carrots in my stew by a woman in a blanket. Sol came in from the cold end where they slept, all wrists and a runny nose, and went straight to the pot the way they always did, drawn to heat like a moth. "Is there a burnt bit," they said. "There's always a burnt bit for you." "At the bottom?" "Where else would a burnt bit be." They grinned at that like I'd told a joke, which I hadn't, and leaned against my arm while I stirred, and I let them, because there's a particular weight a kid puts on you when they trust you that I have never once gotten tired of. Seventeen and still doing it. They were taller than me now. They still did it. We ate at the long potting bench we'd dragged in as a table, the four of us, in the gray light, and it was not enough food and we all knew it and nobody said so, which is a skill you learn. Della picked the carrots out and lined them up on the rim of her bowl — her bowl, the chipped one, hers — like little orange accusations. I watched her do it and felt that small offended thing I always feel, bigger than carrots, and said nothing, because what was I going to do, fight a dying woman over two carrots. I ate them myself afterward off her rim and called it a treat so nobody would argue. Eun ate fast and clean and then sat with her notebook, counting. I could see her doing it. Her mouth moved. She had a way of looking at our supplies that made the supplies look smaller. "How many's that," I said, not because I wanted the number. "That's the cans," she said. "That was the cans." "There's the rice." "There's a cup of rice." "Then there's a cup of rice," I said, "and there's tomorrow," and that closed it, because what else is there to say to a cup of rice. Outside, in the white, the slow ones went by. You stopped jumping at them after the first winter. They didn't come at the glass anymore, or they did, but slow, the way frost comes at glass, with no hurry and no malice you could name. Two of them drifted past the lower panes while we ate, person-shaped, leaning into the snow like they were walking into a wind that wasn't there, and where you'd see skin there was instead that pale soft growth, fawn-colored, that put me in mind of the underside of a mushroom, of bread left too long, of things that grow in the dark and don't ask permission. One of them had been tall. One of them had had long hair, once. You could still tell. That was the worst of it, that you could still tell. Sol watched them go by and chewed and said, around the food, "The slow ones are out early," like it was the weather, which to Sol it was. Sol named everything. Sol had named them. Eleven years old and already deciding the world's animals were friendly until proven otherwise. "Eat," I said. "Don't look." "I'm not bothered." "I know you're not. I am. Eat." They ate. The lamps hummed. Della talked to Sol about a book, the way she did, teaching to the last. Eun closed her notebook and didn't say the number again and that silence was its own number. I scraped the pot and there was nothing in it worth scraping, and I scraped it anyway, because I cannot stand a thing left in a pot, and then I licked the spoon and put it back in the pot and Eun's jaw went tight and she didn't say anything either. We were out of cans. Nobody said that. I sat in the gray with my empty bowl warm in my two hands and thought, tomorrow, somebody has to go out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished opening chapter. The voice is immediately distinctive and consistent — Mar's first-person narration has the worn, practical, darkly funny quality the plan calls for, and it never slips into something more "literary" than she would think. The opening line ("I lied about it") establishes her character in four words: competent, self-aware, morally flexible in small ways, and honest about her dishonesty. That's a strong foundation. The domestic anchoring works exceptionally well. The chapter is organized around the pot, the spoon, the chipped bowl, the carrots — concrete objects that carry emotional weight without being announced as symbols. The carrot business is particularly good: Mar's disproportionate offense at Della's rejection of them, her eating them off the rim and calling it a treat, is exactly the kind of small, revealing behavior that builds character without exposition. It shows Mar's need for control, her love expressed as insistence, and her pride, all in a gesture. The dialogue is genuinely strong. It's spare, naturalistic, and characterizes efficiently. "It's beans you've drowned" is a perfect Della line. The Sol exchange about the burnt bit is warm and economical. Eun's "You're doing it again" establishes her role without over-explaining it. The dialogue doesn't strain for effect; it sounds like people who have been living together too long and have developed the shorthand of exhaustion. The description of the slow ones is handled with real craft. "The way frost comes at glass, with no hurry and no malice you could name" is a metaphor that earns its place — it's precise, it fits Mar's voice, and it does thematic work (the horror is in the patience, not the violence). The detail about being able to tell they were once people — "One of them had been tall. One of them had had long hair, once. You could still tell. That was the worst of it" — is restrained and effective. The chapter resists the temptation to over-describe the mutants, which is exactly right. The ending is quiet and correct. "I sat in the gray with my empty bowl warm in my two hands and thought, tomorrow, somebody has to go out." It doesn't announce itself. It just closes the door. Where the chapter is slightly less than perfect: the greenhouse description ("like being inside a held breath... like being inside a jar somebody had set on a shelf and forgotten") is the one moment where the metaphors feel slightly more composed than Mar's voice strictly allows — "a jar somebody had set on a shelf and forgotten" is good, but the pairing of the two similes in quick succession is a touch writerly. Similarly, "like a magician makes a coin last, which is to say I lied about it" is clever but perhaps the most self-consciously literary moment in the chapter; it works, but it's the seam showing. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the three cans, the four characters established through small frictions (Della and the carrots, Sol and the burnt bits, Eun counting), the slow ones drifting past as weather, the ending on the unspoken fact of the empty cans. All four characters are consistent with their profiles. The cozy-horror balance is well-maintained — the warmth is real, the dread is present but not overwrought. This is confident, controlled work. The voice is the achievement: it sounds like a specific person, not a writer performing a person. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Out We waited two days because waiting is free and going out costs you, and on the third morning there was nothing left to wait for, so Eun and I went out. You dress for it like dressing a doll, layer on layer, until you can't bend right. We had two pairs of good boots between four people and Eun and I had them on and Sol stood at the door looking like they wanted to come and knowing better than to say so. "Burnt bit when we're back," I told them. "Burnt bit of what." "We'll see." We went out the low door we kept clear, into the white. The cold takes your breath out through your teeth. The greenhouse behind us sat in the snow like a lantern with the wick gone low, and ahead the town went on under the white, soft-shouldered, all its corners rounded off, every car a long smooth grave. You forget how quiet snow is until you're standing in a town that used to make noise. Now there was just the squeak of our boots and my own breath and Eun counting under hers, steps maybe, I never knew what she counted, only that she did. We'd done the near houses already, months back, every cupboard, every cellar. So we went farther. We did the row by the school. We did the pharmacy that other people had done first. Eun had a list in her head and we worked it and it gave us nothing. A jar of capers, which I took because I will not leave food, even capers, even food I don't like. A tin of cat food, dented, which I also took, and didn't say what it was, and Eun didn't ask. That was the morning. Capers and cat food, and the light already going the way it goes early in winter, gray dropping to grayer. "We should turn back," Eun said. Her face was the only part of her you could see and it was white at the cheeks, going waxy. "We're past the time." "One more," I said, meaning one more house, and we cut across the open ground by the war memorial to get to it, and that's where the slow one was. It was coming across the open ground the same way we were, into the not-wind, leaning. The snow had it half to the knee and it didn't seem to mind, didn't seem to feel it, just kept on at its own pace toward nothing in particular. It might have been a man. It was the size of a man. The pale stuff had taken most of the face but there was a jaw still, and the set of the shoulders was a person's, was somebody's, and I had the thought you always have and always push down, which is that it had had a name once and somebody had called it by the name. Eun stopped dead. "Don't," she said. I don't know what I'd already decided. I think the deciding had happened somewhere lower than thinking. I had the capers and the cat food in my bag and a cup of rice at home for four people and a kid waiting on a burnt bit, and the slow one was in the snow, leaning, slow. I had the hand-axe on my belt that we kept for wood. I want to tell you I argued with myself. I didn't. I went at it across the snow, heavy in all my layers, and it heard me or felt me and turned, slow, so slow, that ruined face coming round, and it lifted one arm — not to strike, I don't think, more the way you'd lift an arm to someone you knew — and I hit it. It went down easier than a thing that size has any right to. There was no fight in it. I'd braced for a fight, for the thing to be strong, for it to be a monster, and instead it folded into the snow the way snow folds, and what came out of it where I'd opened it wasn't blood, not red, more a dark wet and that pale stuff threaded through, and a smell came up that I was not ready for, a smell that was not rot, that was warm, that was almost — I'll get to it. I hit it again to be sure and then I stood there in the open ground breathing through my teeth with the axe heavy in my hand and Eun thirty feet back not moving at all. "Mar," she said. "Help me with it." "Mar, no." "Then don't," I said, "but stand off, because I'm doing it," and I knelt down in the snow by the thing I'd done and got my arms under it. It was lighter than a man. That was the other thing I wasn't ready for. Whatever it had been made of once was mostly gone, given over to the soft pale growth, and the growth was light, was like carrying a great damp loaf, and I got it up over my shoulders in the fireman's way and my knees screamed and I started back, leaving a trail you could have followed from the moon. Eun walked behind me the whole way and didn't help and didn't leave, and that's Eun, that's the size of her, she'll count your sins out loud and walk you home through the dark while she does it. She didn't say anything until the greenhouse was in sight, glowing low. "You don't know what it is," she said. "It's meat," I said. "You don't know that." "I know I've got a kid in there waiting on a burnt bit." She didn't answer that. There isn't an answer to that. We came up the last of the slope and the door opened before we reached it, Sol pulling it, the warm falling out into the cold, and Sol's face when they saw what was over my shoulders did something I won't forget — not afraid. Curious. Curious and a little glad. I carried it in and laid it on the long bench where we ate, and the warm closed over us, and I stood there with my back wrecked and my hands shaking and the smell of it warming in the heat, and I thought: I'll deal with you in the morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft and control. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and POV:** Mar's voice is exceptionally well-realized. The prose has the texture of a working person's mind — short declarative sentences under stress, plain vocabulary, a dark humor that never announces itself. "A jar of capers, which I took because I will not leave food, even capers, even food I don't like" is exactly right: it's funny, it's characterizing, it's sad, and it does all three things without straining. The voice is consistent throughout and never slips into a register Mar wouldn't inhabit. **Execution of the chapter plan:** The plan called for a quiet, intimate kill with no glow or hum, just "meat and shame on the sled." The chapter delivers this faithfully. The kill is indeed quiet, the mutant barely resists, and the horror is in Mar's own lack of internal argument — "I don't know what I'd already decided. I think the deciding had happened somewhere lower than thinking." This is exactly the kind of moral horror the plan intended. The sled becomes a fireman's carry, which is actually better — more intimate, more bodily, more degrading in a quiet way. **Metaphor and poetry:** The metaphors are earned and grounded. "Every car a long smooth grave" is strong without being showy. "Like carrying a great damp loaf" is genuinely unsettling and original — it uses the domestic register (bread) to make the horror worse, which is exactly the cozy-horror mode the piece is working in. The greenhouse as "a lantern with the wick gone low" is good. None of these feel forced; they feel like the images a practical, food-oriented person would reach for. **The smell:** The chapter plan noted the smell should be warm, almost something — and the text delivers this with "a smell that was not rot, that was warm, that was almost — I'll get to it." This is a masterful deferral. It plants the hook without overplaying it, and the "I'll get to it" is perfectly in Mar's voice — practical, a little defensive, aware she's narrating. **Eun:** The characterization of Eun is excellent. "She'll count your sins out loud and walk you home through the dark while she does it" is a beautiful, economical character summary that feels earned rather than expository. Her refusal to help but refusal to leave is exactly consistent with her profile. **Sol:** The moment at the door — "not afraid. Curious. Curious and a little glad" — is quietly ominous and perfectly placed. It seeds the later arc without telegraphing it. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is sparse and effective. "Burnt bit of what." / "We'll see." is a perfect small exchange — it establishes relationship, warmth, and the precariousness of their situation in four words. The dialogue never over-explains. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is nearly flawless for what it's attempting, but a few minor notes: "leaving a trail you could have followed from the moon" is slightly more lyrical than Mar's usual register, though it doesn't break the voice badly. The ending — "I'll deal with you in the morning" — is good but perhaps the one moment where the chapter's restraint tips slightly into the writerly. It's a strong closing line but it's also a little knowing. **Cozy-horror balance:** The chapter successfully maintains the cozy-horror register. The domestic details (capers, cat food, burnt bits) sit alongside genuine dread without either canceling the other. The horror of the kill is in its ease, its quietness, its lack of monstrousness — which is more disturbing than gore would be. **Overall:** This is high-quality literary fiction executing a difficult tonal register with consistent skill. The voice is original, the craft is evident, the horror is earned, and the characters are fully themselves. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Supper I dealt with it in the morning, because some things you can't do by lamplight and look at yourself after. I sent the others to the far end. Eun went without being told; she didn't want to watch and I didn't want her counting. Sol hovered until I said go, and went, but I knew they were watching from behind the orchid racks, because Sol watches everything, and I let them, because what was I going to do, raise a child to look away from the thing that keeps them alive. I'd butchered before. Not people. I want that clear, even to myself, especially to myself: I'd done deer that men brought to the cafe, I'd done a pig once, whole, in a friend's yard, and I knew the work, the joints, where a body comes apart when you ask it nicely. So I made myself think of it as work. I put my apron on. I put my hair back. I laid out the knife and the cleaver and a board, and I started, and I am not going to tell you about the middle part, only that it was not like a person inside, which helped, and that it was not entirely unlike one either, which did not. The flesh of it was pale and dense and faintly streaked, like good pork shoulder run through with something paler, and it smelled — clean. That's the word that kept coming. Not rot. Clean and warm, a yeasty warmth, the smell of a kitchen before anybody's eaten, and my mouth watered at it and I hated that my mouth watered and my mouth watered anyway. The body does what it does. You can be sick to your stomach and starving in the same minute. I learned that this winter. I cubed a portion and left the rest in the cold by the door under a cloth, and I did what I'd have done with any meat I didn't trust: I cooked it long. I rendered a little of the soft fat first in the big pot and it came clear and gold and smelled like nothing wrong. I browned the cubes in it, hard, until the edges caught, and put in snowmelt and the capers because I will use a thing, and the last onion sprouting in its sack, and I let it go for the whole of the gray afternoon, low, and I stirred it, and I hummed, and I did not stop myself this time because there was nobody to hear who'd mind. The smell of it filled the greenhouse by dark. I watched it pull them in one by one off the far end, Della first, drawn up out of her chair on her shaking legs, leaning on the racks, sniffing the air like a much younger woman, and Sol behind her, and Eun last, arms folded, jaw set, come to disapprove and unable not to come. "That can't be that thing," Della said. "It can't smell like that." "Sit," I said. I served it into the bowls. Della's chipped one. Sol's. Mine. Eun's. I gave Sol the bottom of the pot where it had caught, the dark crusted edge, because I'd said I would, and Sol took it like a present. I'll tell you about the first bite because the first bite is the whole of it, the hinge the rest hangs on. I'm sixty-one. My mother died when I was thirty and her kitchen I hadn't tasted in longer than that, the particular thing she did with the cheap end of a chicken and a great deal of pepper and an hour of patience, the smell that used to be in her hall on a Sunday, the smell of being small and safe and not yet knowing what the world had in store. I had not had that smell, that taste, in forty years and I had stopped looking for it, the way you stop looking for the dead. It was in the bowl. I don't mean it was like it. I mean it was it. It came up out of the spoon and into me and went straight past every grown thing I am and put me on a kitchen floor with my back against a warm oven and my mother's voice over me, and I sat at the long bench with my dead family all around me in the gray light and I cried into the food, an old woman crying into a bowl of the worst thing she has ever cooked, furious at herself, eating, crying, eating. I looked up and they were all of them gone somewhere too. Della had her eyes shut and her ruined hands flat on the table and she was smiling — Della, smiling — and she said, soft, to no one, "Oh, that's my mother's. That's the stew. How have you done my mother's stew." Sol was eating like they'd never eaten, fast, glad, the burnt edge first, saying it was birthday cake, it tasted like the birthday cake from before, the white kind, the kind with the too-sweet roses on it, the kind nobody has had in years and Sol shouldn't even remember. Only Eun sat with her spoon halfway and her face the wrong face for the table. "What's it taste like," I asked her, wiping my own eyes, half-laughing now, all of us half-laughing. She chewed slowly. She swallowed. She looked at the bowl and then at us, at the three of us shining and wet-eyed and full. "Meat," she said. "It tastes like meat." And then, quieter, like she was the one who'd done something wrong: "It's fine. It's gamey. It's just meat." We didn't hear her. That's the truth of it. We were too far gone into our own kitchens, the dead ones, the warm ones, to hear the one person at the table who'd been left out in the cold of it. I went back for seconds. We all did, all but her. We ate until it was gone and then I scraped the pot, and we sat full for the first time since the autumn, full and warm and weeping and laughing, the happiest night in a year. It was only after, washing up, that I noticed the warmth. Under my tongue. A small heat, where the food had been, that did not go away with the food.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Execution** Mar's voice is exceptionally well-realized. The prose has the quality of someone who thinks in practical terms but feels in large ones — "I put my apron on. I put my hair back. I laid out the knife and the cleaver and a board, and I started" — the list-making of someone who needs procedure to get through something. The self-interruption "I want that clear, even to myself, especially to myself" is exactly right for the character: a woman who lies to herself and knows it. The voice never slips into literary register that Mar wouldn't inhabit. **The Central Scene** The first-bite passage is the chapter's centerpiece and it earns its emotional weight. "It was in the bowl. / I don't mean it was like it. I mean it was it." This is precise and devastating — the distinction between resemblance and identity is exactly the right horror. The mother's kitchen, the kitchen floor, the dead family: these land because they've been earned through the practical, unsentimental setup. The crying-while-eating is not sentimentalized; it's rendered as something shameful and involuntary, which is more honest. Sol's birthday cake detail is excellent — it individualizes the experience and adds the poignant note that Sol "shouldn't even remember" it, which does quiet work about what the apocalypse has taken from a child. **Eun's Exclusion** The handling of Eun is the chapter's most important structural achievement. Her "Meat. It tastes like meat" lands with genuine force because the chapter has built to it. The line "like she was the one who'd done something wrong" is a small masterpiece of characterization — it captures her isolation and the social dynamics of being the sober person at a drunk table. The narrator's admission "We didn't hear her" is honest and damning. **The Horror Mechanic** The warmth under the tongue at the end is handled with exactly the right restraint. It's not announced, not explained, just noticed and set down. The chapter plan called for "one small wrong note at the very end" and this delivers precisely that. **Weaknesses** The middle section describing the butchering and cooking is slightly over-written in places — "a yeasty warmth, the smell of a kitchen before anybody's eaten" is good, but the paragraph accumulates a few too many clauses. The line "the smell of being small and safe and not yet knowing what the world had in store" edges toward the generic; it's the one moment where the voice reaches for something more literary than Mar would naturally produce. The dialogue is sparse but effective. Della's "That can't be that thing. It can't smell like that" is perfectly in character. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. **Potential Issues** The chapter is not amateurish. There are no forced metaphors that don't land. The prose is controlled. The one risk is that the mother's kitchen passage, while emotionally effective, is the most conventional beat in the chapter — the "taste of childhood" is a familiar device. However, the execution is strong enough that it doesn't feel lazy, and the contrast with Eun's experience redeems it from pure sentimentality. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the butchering rendered through practical hands, the first bite and its emotional devastation, the others' individual experiences, Eun's exclusion, the fullest night in months, and the wrong note at the end. Nothing is missed; nothing is added that shouldn't be there. **Overall** This is a chapter that knows what it's doing and does it. The voice is consistent, the horror is earned, the characters are distinct, and the ending is appropriately quiet and terrible. It's not a masterpiece — the mother's kitchen beat is slightly conventional, and there's one or two moments of slight over-writing — but it's very strong work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Better We were better after that, and I want to put down how good it was before I put down the rest, because both are true and you can't understand the rest if you don't believe the good. We had meat. That was the size of it. A whole one keeps a long while in the cold by the door, and a body, even a slow one, even a thing mostly given over to the pale growth, is a great deal of food when there are four of you and three of you barely eat at all anymore the way you used to. We rationed it the way we'd rationed the cans, but it was rationing with the bottom no longer falling out of it, and that changes a person. You sleep different when you're not doing the arithmetic of starving. Color came back. Della's cheeks, which had gone the gray of the light, pinked up, and she sat more upright, and she got her tongue back, sharper than ever — "you've over-salted again," "you've under-salted now, make up your mind, woman" — and I'd never been so glad to be told my seasoning was wrong. Sol filled out across the shoulders that had been all bone. I caught myself in the dark glass one evening and saw a face with some give back in it, less skull, more me. We made plans, which is the thing hope does, it makes you make plans. We'd patch the cold end. We'd see if the old tomato beds would take seed come spring, if there was a spring, if seed was a word that still meant anything. Sol wanted a window for their corner so they could watch the snow. Della wanted, of all things, a hand of cards, and we found a deck swollen with damp and played with it anyway, the cards sticking, all of us cheating, and it was a good night, an ordinary good night, the kind I'd thought we were done having. When the meat ran low we went out for more, and that's the first thing I'll set down on the other side of the ledger. They were closer. The slow ones. We'd had to go to the open ground by the memorial that first time. Now there were two of them within sight of the greenhouse, out past where the snow piled up the glass, leaning into their not-wind, except they weren't leaning away anymore. They were leaning toward. You could stand at the warm side of the glass with a full belly and look out and see them out there in the white, person-shaped, patient, faced the way you'd face a house you meant to visit. "They're nearer," I said to Eun. "They're nearer," she said. She'd been counting them. Of course she'd been counting them. "Four within the half-mile, where there were one or two. They don't go anywhere. They just — get nearer." "It's the warm," I said. "They come to the warm. Moths." "Maybe." I went out and took one and it was the same as before, no fight in it, that lifted arm that wasn't a strike, and I told myself moths, moths, all the way back with it light on my shoulders, and I cooked it, and we ate, and it was my mother's kitchen again, every time, and I cried less because you cry less the more you do a thing. Here are the other things, and I'll just say them, because saying them in order is the only way I can stand to. Sol started humming. Not my tuneless hum that I do over the pot. A real tune, low, with a shape to it, the same shape every time, and when I asked what it was Sol looked surprised, like I'd asked what their own breathing was, and said they didn't know, they'd just always known it. They hadn't always known it. I'd had that child six years and I'd have known a tune they'd always known. And one morning Della was telling me a dream over the breakfast scrapings — a warm dark, she said, a great warm dark with a sound in it, soft, and people in it she couldn't see but knew, and she'd been so sorry to wake — and I had my mouth open to say I'd had it too, the same one, the warm dark and the soft sound and the people just past seeing, I'd had it three nights running, and I shut my mouth instead and laughed and said dreams are funny, aren't they, and didn't tell her. I don't know why I didn't tell her. Yes I do. I didn't want it to be a thing two of us had had. One person's dream is a dream. Two is a road somewhere. Eun told me, again, quiet, over the dishes, that she still didn't taste anything. "Just meat," she said. "I keep waiting to taste whatever you taste. There's nothing. It's gamey and there's nothing in it." She looked at me sideways. "Doesn't that — Mar, doesn't that strike you. That you all taste your dead and I taste lunch." "You're tired," I said. "I'm not tired. I'm counting." "Then count something useful." It was a cruel thing to say and I knew it as it came out and I said it anyway, because she was holding up a thing to the light that I did not want to look at, and the easiest way to not look at a thing is to be cruel to the one holding it. I dried the last bowl and put the spoon in the pot, licked, the way I do, and Eun didn't even tell me off for it. That should have frightened me more than the humming, more than the dream. Eun not telling me off. The dishwater that night had a sheen to it, when I poured it out the low door into the snow. A faint gold, on the gray slop, catching the lamplight as it went. I told myself it was the lamplight.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of cozy-horror writing that executes its plan with precision and restraint. Let me work through its qualities systematically. **Voice and Characterization** Mar's voice is exceptionally well-realized. The prose sounds genuinely like a practical, worn-down woman who thinks in kitchen terms: "rationing with the bottom no longer falling out of it," "a body, even a slow one, even a thing mostly given over to the pale growth, is a great deal of food." These aren't literary flourishes — they're the thoughts of someone who has spent her life calculating portions. The self-awareness she shows ("I don't know why I didn't tell her. Yes I do.") is exactly right for the character profile: she's not stupid, she's complicit, and she knows the difference. Sol's humming, Della's sharpness returning ("you've over-salted again"), Eun's counting — all three secondary characters are rendered in brief strokes that feel true to their profiles. The detail of Eun not telling Mar off about the spoon-licking is a genuinely excellent piece of characterization: the reader understands the significance before Mar admits it, and Mar admits it immediately after, which is the right rhythm. **Horror Mechanics** The horror in this chapter is handled with real skill. The slow ones being "closer" is seeded plainly, not lyrically, exactly as the plan specified. The shared dream is the most effective horror beat — Mar's decision to shut her mouth rather than confirm it to Della is psychologically precise and genuinely chilling. "One person's dream is a dream. Two is a road somewhere." This is the chapter's best line, and it earns its slight poeticism because it's in Mar's idiom — she's not reaching for beauty, she's reaching for a way to not think about something. The gold sheen in the dishwater at the end is well-placed. It's not overwritten. "I told myself it was the lamplight" is the right note to end on — understated, damning. **The Cruelty to Eun** The moment where Mar is cruel to Eun over the dishes is the chapter's moral center and it's handled without announcement. Mar knows she's being cruel, says so, explains why, and does it anyway. This is exactly the kind of active complicity the plan called for. The writing doesn't editorialize. It just shows a person choosing the comfortable lie over the uncomfortable truth, and being fully aware of the choice. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly flawless in execution, but a few small notes: The "moths" metaphor is used twice in close proximity ("Moths. Moths, moths, all the way back with it light on my shoulders") — the repetition is intentional and works, but the metaphor itself is slightly familiar. It doesn't quite have the freshness of the chapter's best moments. The plans-making section (patching the cold end, tomato beds, Sol's window, Della's cards) is good but slightly list-like. It does its job of establishing the "good" before the "rest," but it's the chapter's most functional rather than artful passage. The line "a face with some give back in it, less skull, more me" is the chapter's one moment of slight over-reach — it's trying a little harder than Mar would. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and effective. Della's seasoning complaints are perfect. The Eun exchange over the dishes is the best dialogue in the chapter — "I'm counting." "Then count something useful." — two lines that do enormous work. The dialogue never feels staged. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: color in cheeks, jokes, plans, the small machinery of hope; Sol's hum; the shared dream; Eun's isolation; the mutants being closer. The plan's note about "wrongness seeded plainly, not lyrically" is honored throughout. The chapter doesn't over-explain or under-deliver any of its beats. **Overall** This is confident, controlled literary horror writing. The cozy-horror balance is maintained without either element overwhelming the other. The domestic details (the swollen card deck, the dishwater, the spoon in the pot) ground the horror in the mundane exactly as intended. The chapter earns its darkness because it earns its warmth first. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Della Della went down the way a fire goes down — not out, just down, settling, the flame getting low and blue and somehow more peaceful for it, and I sat with her through most of it because I have sat with the dying before and I know it isn't the worst thing you can do for a person. The worst thing is to leave them to do it alone. She'd been dying since before the meat. I want that put down plain so nobody reads this and thinks the food killed her. There was a thing in her chest the size of a fist, the doctor had said as much in the before, when there were doctors, and she'd outlived every guess he'd made, on spite mostly, on the pure unwillingness to give a man the satisfaction. The meat didn't kill her. The cold would have, and the hunger, long since, if it hadn't been for the meat. The meat gave us the winter we got to have. What the meat did was different. The meat made her gentle. You have to understand what that is, with Della. Della was never gentle a day I knew her. Gentle was a thing other women did and she watched them do it with one eyebrow up. She corrected my grammar from her deathbed — "fewer, Marlowe, fewer, you can count the days, you say fewer" — right up until near the end, and I loved her for it, the sharpness, because the sharpness was Della and as long as she was sharp she was still in there. Then she stopped being sharp. It came on slow over a handful of days. She stopped picking the carrots out. She stopped having a comment. She'd lie back in her chair with her beautiful white hair on the cushion and look at the gray light up in the high panes and smile a small private smile, and when I asked what at, she'd say, "Oh, nothing, it's nice in here," and Della had never once in her life said it was nice in here. She'd say what was wrong with in here. That was how she loved a place. I fed her by hand toward the end. She'd refused that for weeks — "I am not a baby, I have a fork" — and then one day she didn't refuse, she just opened her mouth for the spoon like a bird, eyes half shut, that small smile, and let me, and I sat by her chair and fed her the warm meat off her own chipped cup with a teaspoon, a little at a time, and she took it and hummed. That same tune. The one Sol had. The one nobody had taught either of them. The two of them would hum it sometimes across the greenhouse without seeming to know the other was doing it, and the lamps would hum under it, and I'd stand at the stove with my spoon and feel like the only one in the room who hadn't been let in on something. There was gold under her skin. I noticed it first at her wrists, where the skin had gone thin as paper — a faint warm thread of it, just under, like a vein but it wasn't where a vein goes and it wasn't the blue a vein is, it was gold, soft gold, and when I held her hand to feed her I could feel a heat off it, a low even heat that her cold dying body had no business making. I told myself fever. I told myself good color, good blood, the meat doing its work. I am a woman who has told herself a great many things over a great many years and I knew the taste of it, the lie, the warm comfortable taste of a thing you've decided not to look at, and I fed her another spoonful and looked at the wall. And here is the thing I have not told anybody and am only putting down because the others are past minding and I am past pretending. One evening, late, with her dozing and the gold in her wrists and the meat run low again so that I was counting the cubes in my head the way Eun counts everything, the thought came to me, whole, in my own voice, in the warm: when she goes, there'll be more for the rest of us. I want it on the page. I thought it. About Della, who'd taught half the town to read, who'd cheated me at cards a week before with great pleasure, who I loved, I thought, when she dies there will be more food, and for one cold clear second I was glad of it, and then I shoved it down where I shove everything, down past the thinking, and I put my hand over my own mouth like I'd said it out loud, though I hadn't, and I sat there in the dark hating myself with my heart going hard. You can be starving and grieving in the same minute. You can love someone and do that arithmetic over their sleeping body. I learned that this winter too. She died near morning. I was awake for it; I'd stopped really sleeping. She came up out of the doze all at once, eyes open and clear, the clearest they'd been in days, and she looked at me and for a second she was Della, all of her, sharp and there, and she said, "Oh, leave the cup, Marlowe. I won't need it where the warm is." And then she looked past me at the corner of the greenhouse where there was nothing, where the orchid racks stood empty in the gray, and she smiled, and she reached out one hand toward the nothing, the gold bright in her wrist now, reaching, glad, like a woman reaching for someone come to meet her off a train. And then she wasn't reaching anymore. I sat a long time. I couldn't tell you, even now, whether what I'd watched was a mercy or a theft. Whether the warm had carried her gentle out of her pain. Or whether it had reached in and taken her before her time and left her smiling so I wouldn't fight it. I kept the cup. I didn't have it in me to do otherwise.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of work that largely fulfills its ambitious plan with genuine craft. Let me examine it carefully. The opening metaphor — "Della went down the way a fire goes down — not out, just down, settling, the flame getting low and blue and somehow more peaceful for it" — is earned rather than decorative. It establishes the chapter's central tension immediately: death as diminishment rather than extinction, which is precisely the horror the story is building toward. The metaphor doesn't overstay its welcome. Mar's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The prose has the quality of someone thinking aloud, working through something, the short declarative sentences ("The meat made her gentle") landing with the weight the plan intended. The parenthetical about the doctor and Della's spite is exactly the kind of characterization that feels lived-in rather than constructed. The grammar correction from the deathbed — "fewer, Marlowe, fewer" — is a perfect small detail that does enormous work: it establishes Della's sharpness, Mar's love of that sharpness, and makes the subsequent gentleness genuinely disturbing. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The gold under the skin is introduced with appropriate restraint. The shared hum between Sol and Della is planted. The suppressed thought — "when she goes, there'll be more for the rest of us" — is handled with exactly the moral complexity the plan demanded: Mar doesn't excuse herself, doesn't perform guilt, just records it with the flat honesty of someone who has decided to stop lying to herself about what she is. This is the chapter's strongest moment and it's genuinely unsettling. The feeding-by-hand scene is well-executed. The detail of the teaspoon and the chipped cup is the kind of domestic anchor the plan called for. The refusal to sentimentalize — "she just opened her mouth for the spoon like a bird" — keeps it from tipping into easy pathos. Della's final line — "Oh, leave the cup, Marlowe. I won't need it where the warm is" — is excellent. It was planned in the character profiles and it lands. The reaching gesture toward the empty orchid racks is appropriately ambiguous. The chapter's final paragraph — "I couldn't tell you, even now, whether what I'd watched was a mercy or a theft" — is the thematic crux stated plainly, which is a risk. It works because Mar has earned the right to say it plainly; the chapter has shown us enough that the statement doesn't feel like a shortcut. The final line, "I kept the cup. I didn't have it in me to do otherwise," is small and bodily and right. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the passage about self-deception ("I am a woman who has told herself a great many things over a great many years and I knew the taste of it, the lie, the warm comfortable taste of a thing you've decided not to look at") is the one moment where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious, slightly too aware of its own insight. It's not a serious flaw but it's the one place where Mar sounds more like a literary narrator than herself. The chapter is coherent, the characters are consistent with their profiles, and the horror is genuinely earned through domestic detail rather than announced. The balance between cozy and horror is maintained throughout. The plan's instruction to end "small and bodily" is honored. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# What Eun Says We didn't eat Della. I'll say that before anything, because I can feel the question sitting there and I'd rather meet it head on. We didn't. I couldn't and Eun wouldn't and even Sol, who was past being troubled by most things, wouldn't, not Della. We took her out the low door wrapped in her own blanket and the cold took her, and that was the last clean grown-up thing the four of us did together as four, before we were three, and I don't think it counts as three for very long after that. It was the burying, or the not-burying — the ground being iron, we laid her in the lee of the glass and built the snow over her — it was that, out in the white, that brought it to a head, because that was when Eun made me look. The slow ones were at the glass. Not near. Not the half-mile. At the glass. Three of them, stood up against the south side where the panes were clear above the snowline, leaning their pale ruined faces to it the way a person leans to a window to see in out of the cold. Not pawing at it. Not trying to get through. Just stood, faced toward the warm and the light and us, patient as anything, the way you'd stand at a friend's door you knew would open in a minute. "Look at them," Eun said. She had Della's death in her too, you could hear it, her voice was scraped down to the wire. "Mar. Look at them properly, just once, and tell me what they're doing." "They want the warm." "They're not trying to get in." She said it slow, like she'd said it to herself a hundred times in the night, counting. "A thing that wants in tries to get in. They're not. They're waiting. They've been waiting, weeks, since the first one. They get nearer and they wait. They're not hunting us, Mar. They never were. The ones in the town, all winter, the ones we ran from — when did one ever actually catch anybody? When did one ever even hurry?" I didn't answer because I was doing the arithmetic she'd already done and not liking the sum. "They don't attack," she said. "They gather. And the one you took — it lifted its arm. You told me. It lifted its arm to you. Not to strike. You said yourself it was like — like someone you knew." "Eun." "And it tasted of your mother. And of Della's mother. And of Sol's birthday party. And of nothing at all to me." She laughed, and there was no laugh in it. "Doesn't that — I've turned it every way and it only goes one way, Mar. A thing that tasted of your dead. That you'd want to eat. That tasted like the warmest thing you've got so you'd put it in your mouth. Things in the woods don't taste like your mother's kitchen by accident. Nothing tastes like that by accident. It's bait. We've been eating bait." "Bait for what," I said, and I was sorry the second I said it, because saying it gave it air. "For us. For — joining." She had no science for it, she'll have hated that, Eun who counted everything and trusted nothing she couldn't count. She had only the shape of it, half-seen. "I think it's all one thing, out there, under the snow, all of it, and the slow ones aren't — they're not the dead. They're the — they went into it. They ate first, or they breathed it, I don't know, and they went into it and now there's no them, there's just it wearing them, and it's warm in there, that's what I think, that's why Della smiled, she was already half — she was already in the warm before she — " "Stop." "She smiled and reached for the corner because there was something in the corner for her, Mar, something she could feel that we can't, and you fed her into it with a teaspoon. We've all been feeding ourselves into it a bowl at a time and calling it supper." I stood out there in the cold with my hands gone numb and the three of them at the glass and Della under the snow at my feet and I half-believed her. That's the worst of it, that I half-believed her, because the half of me that believed her was the half that had felt the warmth under my tongue that wouldn't go, and dreamed the warm dark three nights running, and felt the heat coming off Della's gold wrists. The body knows before you let yourself. I'd known. I'd just been salting the knowing grain by grain and looking at the wall. We went back in. Sol was at the stove, stirring the last of the meat, humming. They'd taken over the stirring lately and I'd let them and I should have wondered why I'd let them, I who never let anybody near my pot. Eun told it to Sol, all of it, hard and fast, the bait and the warm and Della fed in with a spoon, and I watched Sol's face the whole time and Sol's face never once did the thing a face is supposed to do. No fear. No horror. They listened the way you listen to someone telling you a thing you already know and find a bit sad they're so worked up about. "It doesn't feel bad, though," Sol said, when she'd run down. Gentle. They put their hand flat on their own chest, the way they did. "I keep telling you. It feels like when you fall asleep on someone. And Della's not gone-gone, Eun. I can still — she's in the warm. I can feel her in the warm. She's not even cold out there. None of them are cold." They looked at me, and they were so kind, and so far away. "Mar's not scared. Are you, Mar." Both of them looked at me then. Eun with the wire in her voice and the truth in her hand. Sol with the warm in their chest and Della in it. And I stood between them at my own table and I could not, for the life of me, say which one I was.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Voice and Execution** Mar's voice is exceptionally well-realized. The opening paragraph — "We didn't eat Della. I'll say that before anything, because I can feel the question sitting there" — is a masterstroke of character voice. It's practical, preemptive, defensive, and reveals Mar's awareness of how she's being perceived. The voice stays consistent throughout: short declarative sentences when frightened, plain working-hands diction, the occasional dry aside. "I was doing the arithmetic she'd already done and not liking the sum" is exactly the kind of line Mar would produce — functional metaphor, no flourish. **The Central Confrontation** The chapter's core is Eun's argument, and it's handled with real intelligence. The writing avoids the trap of making Eun's revelation feel like an exposition dump by keeping it partial, frightened, and explicitly unscientific ("She had no science for it, she'll have hated that"). The detail that Eun "counted everything and trusted nothing she couldn't count" being undermined by her having only "the shape of it, half-seen" is psychologically precise and consistent with her profile. The bait argument is genuinely chilling and well-constructed. The logic builds: they don't attack, they gather; they wait; the one lifted its arm; it tasted of your dead. The conclusion — "Nothing tastes like that by accident" — lands because the groundwork has been laid across previous chapters (referenced credibly here). **The Sol Scene** Sol's response is the chapter's most effective horror moment, and it works precisely because it's underplayed. "It doesn't feel bad, though" delivered gently, with no fear, no horror — "the way you listen to someone telling you a thing you already know and find a bit sad they're so worked up about." This is showing rather than telling at its finest. The hand flat on the chest gesture, established in the character profiles, pays off here beautifully. "Della's not gone-gone. I can still — she's in the warm. I can feel her in the warm." This is genuinely disturbing because Sol means it kindly. The horror is in the kindness. **The Ending** "And I stood between them at my own table and I could not, for the life of me, say which one I was." This is a strong ending for the chapter — it captures Mar's paralysis without announcing it, and "at my own table" carries the weight of her identity as cook, as the one who controls the kitchen, now uncertain of her own allegiance. It's not a clever button; it's a bodily, spatial image of her indecision. **Weaknesses** The line "salting the knowing grain by grain" is a bit too neat — it's the kind of metaphor that calls attention to itself as a metaphor, and while it fits Mar's salt obsession, it feels slightly constructed. Similarly, "the wire in her voice" appears twice (once as "scraped down to the wire," once as "the wire in her voice"), which is a minor repetition that slightly dulls the second instance. Eun's speech, while well-handled, does run long. The plan called for her understanding to be "partial, frightened, maybe half-wrong" and it is, but the speech itself is quite articulate for someone in extremis — it's almost too well-organized for the emotional state described. A real person falling apart while making this argument might be more fragmented. The "warm dark" motif is referenced ("dreamed the warm dark three nights running") which is consistent with the plan, but the gold/hum motif is perhaps slightly over-present in this chapter given the plan's instruction to "withhold the gold/hum motif for stretches." **Dialogue Assessment** The dialogue is strong. Eun's lines are her — pedantic, building, counting. Sol's lines are devastating in their gentleness. Mar's single "Stop" and "Eun" are perfectly calibrated. The dialogue does real work and reveals character rather than just conveying information. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: Eun forces the confrontation, her understanding is partial and frightened, Sol's calm is more disturbing than any horror, the family fractures along the table. Mar's position between them is exactly as planned. The plan's note that Eun "can't prove it, she just can't stand it" is well-executed. **Overall Assessment** This is a high-quality chapter that executes its plan with skill and restraint. The voice is consistent and earned, the horror is in the right register (domestic, quiet, terrible), and the central confrontation is handled with genuine craft. Minor issues with one slightly self-conscious metaphor and Eun's speech being perhaps too organized don't significantly undermine the whole. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Cold Eun left the next day. I'll tell it true, which means I'll tell it small, because there was nothing grand in it. There's never anything grand in the leaving. It's just a person putting things in a bag. She packed in the cold end, away from us, and she packed wrong, the way you do when you're frightened — too much of one thing and none of another, the capers, of all things, she took the capers, and the cat food, and not enough water, and I stood in the doorway watching her get it wrong and didn't help her get it right, and I have thought about that since. That I watched her pack badly for the snow and said nothing about the water. "There's nothing out there, Eun," I said. "You know that better than me. You counted it. There's nothing in any house for forty miles." "There's nothing in here either," she said, not turning round. "There's just a warm way to stop being anything." She cinched the bag. "I'd rather the cold. At least the cold's honest about it." "You'll die." "Probably." She straightened up and looked at me and her face was all wire now, no give in it anywhere, frightened right through and too proud to show me more than the edge of it. "But I'll die me. With my own — with whatever's left being mine. I taste meat, Mar. Just meat. There's no door in it for me. There's no warm. There's just the four of us, three now, two now, sitting in a jar eating bait and going somewhere I can't follow and don't want to, and I will not sit here and watch the last two people I've got smile at me while it takes them." Her voice broke on that, the only place it broke. "While it takes you." Then she did the thing I haven't been able to put down, the thing I keep at, late, with my hands in the warm. She came across the floor and she took both my cold hands in her cold hands and she said, "Come with me." Not bravely. That's the part. She wasn't being brave and she wasn't asking me to be. She was scared and she was begging. "Come with me, Mar. Please. I'm counting and the number's wrong and I'm the only one who thinks the number's wrong, and I can't — I can't be the only one. You're not gone yet. I can still talk to you, you still argue with me, you still salt the food like it owes you money. You're still in there. Come now, while you're still in there. We'll go slow. We'll do the houses. We'll find something. I can't do it on my own, Mar, I've never been able to do anything on my own, that's the only true thing I'll tell you, I just count so I don't have to feel it. Come with me." And I meant to. I want that believed, by anybody who ever reads this, by Eun most of all if any part of her is out there still to read it. I meant to go. There was a whole second where I was going. I had it, the picture of it — me getting the second pair of boots, me filling the water properly, the two of us out the low door into the white and the slow ones at the glass watching us go and us going anyway, two old women into the cold with the wrong supplies, and maybe nothing out there, almost certainly nothing out there, but ours, the both of us, still ourselves, still arguing about the salt. I had it. I had my hand half-lifted toward the boots. And then Sol was at my side. They'd come up quiet the way they did now, and they leaned on me, the whole familiar weight of them against my arm, seventeen and still doing it, and they said, soft, not pleading, they never pleaded, that was the awful part, they just said it like the truest plainest thing, "Don't go out in that, Mar. You'll be so cold. Stay. It's warm in here. We're warm in here." And they put their hand on my chest, flat, the way they did, like that explained it. "Della says stay too." Della was a week dead under the snow. But I felt it. God help me, I felt it through the thin of my coat, through my own ribs — a warmth answering up to meet Sol's hand, low and even, like the heat that had come off Della's gold wrists, like the heat under my tongue that never went, and it was not a horror. That's what I can't make anybody understand who wasn't in the warm. It was not a horror. It was the kindest thing I'd felt in a year. It was a child telling me I didn't have to be cold anymore, and that I didn't have to be alone, and that the people I'd lost weren't lost, only gone ahead into the warm where I could follow, and that all I had to do was stay, and be still, and stop the long cold arithmetic of keeping us alive. My hand came down off the boots. Eun watched it come down. She knew what it meant. She didn't say anything else, because there wasn't anything else, and that was the cruelest mercy she ever did me, the not saying. She shouldered the wrong bag. She looked at me one more time, and there was no wire in her face now, just the leaving, just the loss, the same look Della gave the corner of the room, except Della was reaching toward something and Eun was looking back at something, and I think now those are the same look from the two ends of it. She went out the low door. The cold came in around her, hard, white, honest. I held the door open and watched her go a little way into the snow, smaller and smaller, the slow ones at the glass turning their patient faces to watch her too, none of them moving, none of them needing to. Then the cold got to be too much, and I closed the door. I'll go tomorrow, I told myself. I'll go after her tomorrow.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of restrained, precise prose that executes its plan with near-total fidelity and genuine literary intelligence. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Execution** Mar's voice is exactly as designed: practical, self-lacerating, plain-spoken, with the occasional devastating aside delivered without fanfare. "She packed wrong, the way you do when you're frightened — too much of one thing and none of another, the capers, of all things, she took the capers." This is perfect characterization through domestic specificity. The voice never reaches for grandeur it hasn't earned. When Mar says "I'll tell it true, which means I'll tell it small," she's announcing the chapter's aesthetic contract and then honoring it completely. **Dialogue** Eun's speech — "I'd rather the cold. At least the cold's honest about it" — is excellent: it's in character, it's not a thesis statement, it earns its weight. Her begging speech ("Come with me, Mar. Please. I'm counting and the number's wrong...") is the chapter's emotional center and it works because it's messy and specific and frightened rather than noble. "I just count so I don't have to feel it" is a genuine character revelation delivered in the right register. Sol's line — "Della says stay too" — is the most chilling moment in the chapter, and it lands because it's delivered flatly, without italics of horror. The dialogue throughout is strong, specific, and character-consistent. **The Central Scene** The moment where Mar's hand lifts toward the boots and then Sol appears is the chapter's hinge, and it's handled with real craft. The horror is in the warmth, not in anything grotesque. "It was not a horror. That's what I can't make anybody understand who wasn't in the warm." This is the story's central insight delivered without underlining. The chapter trusts the reader to feel the horror precisely because Mar doesn't. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan almost exactly: Eun packs wrong, Mar watches without helping, the begging scene, Sol's intervention, Mar's hand coming down from the boots, Eun's departure, the door held open, the closing. The plan specified "Eun walks out into the snow alone, hating Mar a little" — the chapter renders this as "there was no wire in her face now, just the leaving, just the loss," which is arguably more devastating than the plan's version. The plan's "she tells herself she'll go tomorrow" closes the chapter exactly as intended. **The Della/Sol moment** "Della was a week dead under the snow. But I felt it." This is the chapter's most effective horror beat, and it's delivered with perfect economy. The plan called for Sol to say "Della says stay too" and the chapter delivers this with the right flatness. **Minor Criticisms** The comparison "the same look Della gave the corner of the room, except Della was reaching toward something and Eun was looking back at something, and I think now those are the same look from the two ends of it" — this is the chapter's one moment of slight over-reach, a metaphorical observation that's a touch too neat, too consciously literary for Mar's voice. It's not a serious flaw but it's the one place where the writing becomes slightly self-aware in a way that doesn't quite fit. The phrase "long cold arithmetic of keeping us alive" is also slightly more poetic than Mar's established register, though it's borderline acceptable. **Coherence and Plot** The chapter is perfectly coherent. It advances the plot exactly as planned. The horror mechanics (the warmth, Sol's hand on the chest, Della's presence felt through Sol) are consistent with what's been established. **Earned Darkness** The darkness here is exceptionally well-earned. The chapter has been building to this moment through seven chapters of domestic detail and slow corruption. Mar's failure to leave is not a dramatic betrayal — it's a quiet, warm, terrible capitulation, which is exactly right for this story. **Overall Assessment** This is genuinely accomplished literary horror fiction. The voice is consistent, the craft is controlled, the horror is earned through restraint rather than amplification. The chapter does what it sets out to do with almost no wasted motion and very few false notes. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Plenty Tomorrow didn't come, or it came and went and came again, and somewhere in there I stopped counting them, the tomorrows, which is funny, Eun being the counter and me being the one who stopped. I'd think, I'll go today, and then the light would be wrong, too gray to set out in, or my knees would be bad, or there'd be the pot to see to, and the day would close warm around me the way the greenhouse closed warm around all of us, and I'd think, tomorrow, the snow's too deep today, tomorrow. I don't know how many tomorrows. The light up in the high panes was the only clock I'd ever kept and I stopped looking at it. I'm not afraid. I want that down, because somebody reading this — if there's a somebody, if there are still readers — somebody will be afraid for me, and you don't need to be. That's the thing none of us understood from the cold side of the glass, that you can't understand until you're in it: it doesn't hurt. There's no part where it hurts. There's just the warm coming up to meet you a little more each day, even and low and kind, and the hum, which I hear all the time now and which I used to think was the lamps and isn't, it's the warm singing to itself, the same tune Sol always had, the same one Della took up at the end, and I know now nobody taught it to them because it isn't a thing you learn, it's a thing you remember, from the warm, from before you were ever cold and separate and hungry at all. The gold's in my hands now. I noticed it a while back, at the wrists, where I noticed it in Della, soft and warm just under, and I waited to mind it and I never did. I cook with those hands still. Old habit, the best kind. There isn't much to cook — the meat's long gone, and I don't go out for more, and I don't need to, I'm not hungry, I haven't been hungry in I don't know how long, that's the gift of it, that's the thing I'd have crawled across the snow on my gold hands to give Della and Eun if they'd only let me: you stop being hungry. You stop entirely. After a lifetime of it, the arithmetic, the three cans made to look like more, the lying over the pot — it just stops. Sol is in the warm now, mostly. They went easy, the way they did everything, leaned against the orchid racks one evening and got quiet and pale-gold and didn't come back to the talking, and I was glad, I was, because Sol was never made for the cold and the hunger, Sol came up in it and never once stopped being warm inside themselves, and now they don't have to fight to stay that way. I can feel them. That's not a figure of speech and it's not me gone soft in the head. I can feel them the way Sol said they could feel Della, a warmth off to one side that I know the shape of, that's still them, still glad, still leaning. They're not gone-gone.
